channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Classrooms that collaborate and co-create
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aruna Sankaranarayanan

I teach in a continuing education program for teachers, where some of the assignments involve group work. While most participants work amicably with one another, there is a sizable minority that puts their individual self over the group’s interest. Further, most participants tend to err on the side of politeness, often not asserting their views, afraid of hurting the feelings of fellow participants. As a result, the more dominant ones may hold sway over a group and the output of many groups fails to live up to the collective potential of the cohort. I’m beginning to wonder whether we need to explicitly discuss healthy group dynamics with participants before diving into group assignments.

While formal schooling seems to hone our competitive edge, especially as individuals, many people enter the workforce without adequate guidance on how to work effectively in teams. As most jobs require teamwork, schools should emphasize collaboration along with competition.

I’d also like to point out a common misperception. Working collaboratively does not mean that you don’t compete at all. But you also need to work as a team, putting the group’s interest over and above individual ones. In fact, healthy competition between teams can foster creativity and spur innovation.

Collaboration is the way forward in science

A report published by the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering & Medicine highlights the need for collaboration in the sciences. As many contemporary scientific questions involve transdisciplinary understanding, the need for working with teams, of varying sizes, and degrees of partnership, is becoming increasingly essential. Since no single field or discipline can fully tackle complex issues like climate change, growing antibiotic-resistance, or the responsible regulation of artificial intelligence, the need for teams to work seamlessly with one another couldn’t be more pressing.

“Team science” is growing more prevalent with interdisciplinary collaborations extending beyond STEM fields to public policy, public health, law, education, etc. These cross and interdisciplinary linkages that traverse boundaries and borders provide deeper insights and are more impactful than the siloed approach of researchers working independently.

Further, this has spawned a new discipline, the science of team science to optimize transdisciplinary interactions across all stages of a project right from putting together teams to conceptualizing a common charter to managing a seamless implementation of the various steps to finally communicating the results in a responsible manner.

What teachers can do

A tried-and-tested method of promoting cooperation is the jigsaw classroom, pioneered by psychologist Eliot Aronson, who was asked by the school superintendent to diffuse tensions in classrooms and schools following racial desegregation in Austin, Texas in the early 1970s. Aronson and his team observed children in schools and found that “inter-group hostility was being fueled by the competitive environment of the classroom.” In a typical classroom, students work individually and compete with one another. The teacher asks a question and a handful of students raise their hands, eager to chime in, while the vast majority sit quietly, with some hoping that the teacher will not call on them.

To change the dynamics of the class to become more collaborative, Aronson and his team designed the jigsaw classroom which involves dividing the class into groups of about five or six students each. The teacher picks a leader for each group, initially choosing mature students for this role. Divide the lesson into five or six segments, so that each child in a group has access to only one section. For example, if you are discussing biomes, each student in a group can be given information on one particular habitat.

Provide students with time to read and understand the information given to them. Next, you create temporary ‘expert’ groups by having students join others who have read about the same biome. So, you may have desert, tundra, tropical rainforest, grassland, temperate forest, and marine groups. When students are in their ‘expert’ groups, give them time to go over the main points and to rehearse their presentations with their peers.

Students then return to their original jigsaw groups and share their learnings. The teacher may move around from one group to another, observing student interactions. At the end, all students may take an individual quiz on all the material.

Within a few weeks of using this method, Aronson notes on the website of Jigsaw Classroom, that children learnt that it was in their own self-interests to get along with and encourage members of their group to give their best. While this method brought out the best in the children who were academically proficient, it also motivated students who were struggling with traditional teaching methods to rise to new levels. As a result, all children flowered and the dynamics of the classroom altered. In addition to deeper learning and engagement, students in jigsaw classrooms also “expressed less prejudice and negative stereotyping” writes Aronson. Further, students stand to gain even if they spend only a “small portion of their time” in jigsaw groups.

Though it may not be possible to execute the jigsaw classroom for all lessons, you may actively encourage discussions in small groups and other group projects. However, you need to ensure that the quiet children get a chance to speak and that each group respects divergent viewpoints.

If discussions get heated, you may provide guidelines on how to navigate differences of opinion. When faced with contrarian views, help students keep their emotions in check. Model how to listen and reflect their teammates’ views in a calm, collected tone. Once all students feel heard and understood, they may share their alternative viewpoints without denigrating or attacking each other personally. Being able to argue in a mature and measured manner is a skill that eludes many adults. So, teaching students the art of arguing and working well together can be an invaluable life skill.

With this article, this column, The 7 Cs, which morphed into the 10 Cs, comes to an end. I would like to thank Teacher Plus for supporting this project.

References

  • National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. 2025. The Science and Practice of Team Science. Washington, DC: The National Academies Press. https://doi.org/10.17226/29043.
  • https://www.jigsaw.org/history/

Aruna Sankaranarayanan is the author of Zero Limits and the co-author of Bee-Witched along with Brinda S. Narayan. She blogs at www.arunasankaranarayanan.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aruna Sankaranarayanan
Group assignments often fall short of their promise. Aruna Sankaranarayanan writes about how children can be taught about teamwork, cooperation and collaboration from an early age.

item.title: Learning about conservation in Tamarind Grove
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat

To continue our practical learning, I asked the students to form four groups and discuss the rivers, lakes, forests, and parks they have seen near their hometowns. The Backyard Group also actively joined the college group.

Madhavi said, “Most of these children are from urban areas. They will know many places. What is the use of this activity?”

“Let us see how it goes. Each of us will sit with a group and write down what the students discuss. After half an hour, we will ask them to present what they discussed,” I replied.

“Okay, we can do that. Let us go sit with the students,” Prabhakar said.

After about 40 minutes, Prabhakar said, “Students, please come here. Each group has to speak about the highlights of their discussion.”

A student came forward and said, “I am from Group 1. In our group, most of us are from Bengaluru. We have seen Lal Bagh, Cubban Park, and Sankey Tank. I think it is a big pond. Nandi Hills and Bannerghatta National Park, which have tiger and lion safari. These are the major places. There are also many natural lakes associated with nearby villages like Rachenahalli Lake, Ulsoor Lake, Jakkur Lake, etc.”

Another student came forward and said, “I am from Group 2. We are also from an urban area. But apart from this, some of our group members are from Assam. The Nilachal Hills near Guwahati feature dense forest patches and numerous streams. Assam has a state zoo and a botanical garden. They have seen the Brahmaputra River. Fishing is the main livelihood there.”

The third group talked about the Thiruvanathapuram Botanical Gardens, backwaters, and eco tourism attractions in Kerala. Group 4 presented on the hot, dry landscape of the Kutch region of Gujarat, the Gir forest, Shingoda river, lion safari, and the seashores of Somnath.

“Let us consider that you are working on the People's Biodiversity Register. You will go to a designated village, take the village map, understand the terrain, and start listing the different types of flora and fauna. Then, as per the prescribed format, you will fill in the resource availability, quantity, and associated traditional knowledge after consulting with the local residents,” Prabhakar explained.

“People usually name plants and animals in their local languages. Do we then enter the local name in the Biodiversity Register? There may even be two different names for the same plant. How do we address such challenges?” a student asked.

“Yes, it is necessary to prepare the document in the local language and match the plants and animals with their scientific names. If you check the PBR format, there are separate columns for everything. This will avoid any misrepresentation or repetition,” I said.

“How much time does it take to prepare one such PBR?” Anand asked.

“Maybe a year, because you need to observe the place in different seasons to note what's available in that place,” Prabhakar replied.

“Then we need to visit the place many times! Oh, it is exhaustive work!” Madhavi said.

“True, but it gives thorough information about a place. A few years ago, when I was leading a similar project in a village in Arunachal Pradesh, we identified more than 22 varieties of paddy cultivated in the area. This shows the variation and richness of the biodiversity of a particular crop. Imagine all the other crops, insects, birds, fruits, and vegetables that may be available. There are 31 formats to be filled in the PBR. For example, Format 1: Crop Plants, 15: Domesticated Animals, 24: Ornamental Plants. To know more, you can check the National Biodiversity Authority website1. Look here.” I opened the sample format to show.

“What is this Authority?” a student asked.

“To understand that we need to know a few historical milestones. India was always the center of attraction for resources, so people from the West came to this land to trade. Later, Europeans started ruling in many parts of the world. After two World Wars, many countries declared independence. Since 1947, the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) has provided guidelines for trading commodities among countries. The negotiations on trade and tariffs gave birth to the World Trade Organisation (WTO). WTO's current work comes from the 1986-94 negotiations called the Uruguay Round. WTO and its agreements cover trade in services, and in traded inventions, creations, and designs. Under this, Trade Related Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS) came into effect in 1995. In 1992, there was a Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) at Rio de Janeiro in Brazil. It was called the Earth Summit. The agenda was to introduce an international legal instrument for the conservation and sustainable use of biological diversity. It emphasized the need to share costs and benefits between developed and developing countries, as well as ways and means to support innovation by local people. India was one of the signatories to this convention. The National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) was established in 2003 by the central government to implement India’s Biological Diversity Act 2002 and the Biological Diversity Rules 2004.2 The NBA is a statutory body and performs facilitative, regulatory, and advisory functions for the Government of India on issues of conservation, sustainable use of biological resources, and fair and equitable sharing of benefits of use,” Prabhakar explained.

“So this Tamarind Grove conservation is one of the activities under the NBA guidelines? Oh! There is so much to learn,” a student exclaimed.

“Ma’am, on the website is mentioned a Biodiversity Management Committee (BMC). There is a big list. Can you explain?” a student asked.

“Under the Biological Diversity Act, every local body in the state has to constitute a Biodiversity Management Committee within its area of jurisdiction to promote conservation, sustainable use, and documentation of biological diversity. For example, in a village, BMC should be framed so that members can be involved in the preparation of the PBR and understand local biological diversity to take up further activities. Generally, this committee has about seven members, including women,” I replied.

“Now that you are familiar with each other's regions, you must have noticed a difference in the terrain, like hot open places, seashores, mountain ranges, lakes, and botanical gardens. Natural forests are home to in situ conservation, where plants and animals are conserved in their original habitat. Botanical gardens, man-made lakes, and zoos are places for ex-situ conservation. So, when we discussed the IUCN status of different species, it was about the population of a particular species in its natural habitat. Regarding the population assessment strategy, you can learn more from the website,” Prabhakar opened the website to show.3

I added, “If you look at estuaries, where river water meets the ocean, they host special types of fish. These fish are specially adapted to live in fluctuating salinity. In the same way, river water and sea water harbour different sets of biodiversity. Hot desserts are hosts to dry land flora and fauna. Every place has a unique composition of biodiversity.”

Muniyappa came with a big steel can and said, “I have tamarind juice for you. Our village Ayurvedic doctor prepared it. The doctor will join us in a few minutes.”

Muniyappa and Madhavi served everyone tamarind juice.

“It is tasty, sour, and sweet. A little spicy too. Is there jeera?” Sujatha asked.

“Enjoy the juice. Sudheer doctor, will tell you how to prepare it and its benefits,” Muniyappa replied.

Suggested activities

  1. Name the waterbodies and associated fauna found near your town.
  2. Make a list of plants, animals, and birds by visiting the nearby park.

References

  1. http://nbaindia.org/uploaded/pdf/PBR%20Format%202013.pdf
  2. http://nbaindia.org/uploaded/Biodiversityindia/Legal/33.%20Biological%20Diversity%20Rules,%202004.pdf
  3. https://iucn.org/about-iucn

Nandini D is a botanist who has been involved in teaching and research for more than 25 years. Currently, she is engaged as a consultant for Intellectual Property Rights for Karnataka State Council for Science and Technology, Bengaluru, and is a visiting faculty member at RIWATCH, Roing, Arunachal Pradesh. She can be reached at nandinidholepat@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat
The Backyard Discovery group learns about ecosystems, traditional knowledge, and biodiversity governance in a tamarind grove.

item.title: Blackboard to bot: teaching in India’s AI age
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

Think about it – artificial intelligence (AI) isn’t just science fiction anymore. It’s already part of our daily lives. The playlist that knows exactly which song you want to hear or the chatbot that quickly sorts out your bank query – that’s AI at work. But now we’re talking about something bigger: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). Unlike today’s AI, which is good at one job at a time, AGI aims to think, learn, and adapt across different areas – almost like humans do.

For Indian classrooms, this isn’t just a futuristic idea. It’s a reality that could change how our children learn, how teachers teach, and how education itself is shaped in the years ahead. The children sitting in our classrooms today may one day work alongside AGI as colleagues, collaborators, maybe even supervisors. To prepare them for that, it’s not enough to teach coding or digital skills alone. They’ll also need curiosity, ethics, and critical thinking. As educationist Nitin Vijay reminded students in Pune, “Remain a student forever… the real purpose of education is to teach how to learn.” That spirit of lifelong learning is what will keep our students ready for the AI age.

We’re already seeing signs of this shift. The CBSE has introduced AI as a subject for classes 8 to 10. The National Education Policy (NEP 2020) has also spoken clearly about bringing new technologies into teaching, while making sure access and equity remain priorities. Think tanks like ICRIER have reported that adaptive assessments, AI-based tutoring systems, and administrative tools are being tested in Indian schools. The message is clear: India is willing to experiment, but teachers are the ones who must guide students through it responsibly.

One of the most powerful promises of AI in India is its potential to bridge inequalities. Imagine a child in rural Bihar who struggles with fractions. An AI tutor can identify that exact gap and offer personalized practice – without judgment and without delay. Or think of children whose home language isn’t English or Hindi. AI-powered multilingual platforms can help them learn in their mother tongue, making sure language isn’t a barrier. In many ways, this takes forward Gandhi’s dream of making opportunities accessible to every child in every village.

Indian teachers are already showing what this looks like on the ground. Dinesh Patel, a Kendriya Vidyalaya teacher in Mumbai, created Shalu – a humanoid robot that can teach in nine Indian languages, using scrap materials. In Jhansi, another teacher built a low-cost humanoid robot that not only helped with lessons but also improved school attendance. These examples prove that AI in classrooms doesn’t always need expensive imported software. With creativity and resourcefulness, Indian educators are making AI relatable and useful for their students.

But there are challenges we must face. Many schools still lack the basics – steady electricity, reliable internet, or an adequate number of devices. If AI takes root only in well-funded urban schools, the rural-urban divide will grow wider. Then there’s teacher readiness. For many, AI feels overwhelming or intimidating. Without proper training, AI tools risk becoming gimmicks – or worse, outsourced entirely to private vendors, which could leave teachers sidelined.

Ethics, too, are non-negotiable. AI systems are trained on huge amounts of data, but if that data carries biases, against women, against certain communities, against rural India – AI could end up reinforcing inequality instead of reducing it. The ICRIER report has already warned of risks like bias, misuse of student data, and lack of accountability. And let’s not forget privacy: Who owns the data AI collects from students? How is it stored? Do parents even know what’s happening? These are questions we must address before AI becomes routine in classrooms.

There’s also the cultural question. In India, we’ve always seen teachers as gurus – guides, mentors, moral anchors. Where does that leave us if a robot or chatbot starts “teaching”? Perhaps the answer lies in seeing AI as a tool, not a replacement. As Tagore put it, “The highest education is that which does not merely give us information but makes our life in harmony with all existence.” AI can deliver information quickly. But only a teacher can shape wisdom, values, and compassion. If anything, AI can free teachers from routine tasks so that they can focus on what matters most – guiding, inspiring, and nurturing children.

So how do we move forward? Teachers can start small: explain how YouTube recommends the next video, or how a simple chatbot works. Encourage students to design projects like a school helpdesk chatbot or a translation tool for classmates. These make AI less abstract and more hands-on. Just as important, students must be taught to ask ethical questions: Is this fair? What’s being left out? Who decides what goes into the data? In doing so, classrooms can double as spaces for both technical and moral learning.

Of course, schools will also need clear policies – rules on data privacy, guidelines for AI use, and training programs for teachers. Pilot projects must cover rural as well as urban schools to make sure equity is maintained. Parents too must be part of the journey, because in India, education is always a family aspiration. Without their trust, AI adoption will struggle.

Globally, experts like Ray Kurzweil predict that AGI could arrive by 2030. Leaders like Yoshua Bengio are already focused on making sure AI remains safe and aligned with human values. For Indian classrooms, this may sound distant, but it matters. If tomorrow’s machines can think and act independently, our students must be ready to ask hard questions about fairness, responsibility, and inclusion.

Swami Vivekananda said, “Education is the manifestation of the perfection already in man.” If we keep that vision in mind, then AI will not replace education, it will extend it. The blackboard and the chatbot can coexist: one grounding students in fundamentals, the other opening doors to new possibilities. With creativity, ethics, and care, AI can be more than a fancy add-on. It can become a tool for social transformation.

And who will lead this change? Indian teachers, of course – with their resilience, imagination, and ability to do a lot with very little. Our job is to ensure that the children sitting in our classrooms today don’t just survive in a future shaped by AI, but actually shape that future themselves.

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A GenZ in spirit, with the benefit of hindsight, she uses her 37 years in media and education to help leverage generational diversity at work and at home. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerjasingh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
AI is already shaping our daily lives. But what happens when it enters Indian classrooms in a deeper way? Neerja Singh writes about how teachers, students, and schools can prepare for an AI-driven future.

item.title: From patience to precision: Guiding learners to pay attention to details
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Surabhi Agarwal

It was late evening, and Isha was rushing to help Riti finish her English homework. Ever since Riti started second grade, Isha has been supporting her daughter during homework.

“Read the first question aloud,” she instructed her daughter.

Riti started reading confidently, “Question one… The boy was standing ‘dash’ the umbrella.”

Isha waited patiently and then pointed her finger to the dash, “Which preposition will go here?”

Riti looked at her and answered hesitantly.

“Is it ‘under’…?”

“Correct! Now write it down.”

Riti quickly scribbled ‘unber’ and moved to the next question.

Isha stared at the seemingly innocuous spelling mistake in disbelief. She could see ‘d’ written as ‘b’. She also saw that the letters were floating above and below the line given for writing.

She stared long enough to recall all the parenting books and articles that she’d absorbed since her daughter started school. In that moment, all the worst things she could imagine about her daughter’s learning journey clouded her mind. She turned the pages of her daughter’s notebook for some assurance. She saw multiple places where Riti reversed the letter formations. She also saw many places where Riti wrote outside the margins and randomly capitalized letters in between a word.

Isha composed herself, pointed towards the word ‘unber’ and asked calmly, “Riti, can you spot a mistake in this spelling?” Riti looked at the answer diligently. She re-read the word ‘unber’ and found that she had written ‘b’ instead of ‘d’. At that moment, her eyes widened as she corrected it.

Long after the night was over, Isha could not get rid of the anxiety. Are these harmless errors or something more serious? Should I discuss it with Riti’s teacher? Is she feeling supported in her school? Could it be a learning disorder? Will it affect her performance in other activities too? Am I overthinking? What can I do to help her?

Isha decided she’d read, research, and design a few worksheets and games to help her daughter pay attention to the small details while writing.

Isha’s worries are relatable to any parent, teacher, guardian, or educator who has assisted a child with their written work. Kids under 10 make mistakes with spelling, grammar, and presentation as a natural part of the learning process.

As parents and teachers, we keep wondering about the reasons. “One reason I feel this continues to happen is not giving sufficient time to go back and check the writing. Usually, writing is done and submitted haphazardly,” says Chandrika Shenoy Kamath, a Pune based education consultant who is also a mother to a second grader. While talking about the errors in her son’s written work, she elaborates, “When he has a flow of ideas, he writes continuously without paying attention to these details. When I ask him to go back and reflect, he is able to correct the mistakes by himself.”

While navigating these challenges, parents may begin to worry about inadvertent consequences. They wonder about learning disorders like dyslexia or dysgraphia. Lack of simplified information, guidance, and professional support enhances this confusion.

As schools and students gravitate towards digital platforms for expression, it is crucial to actively bring the learner’s attention to the small details in their handwritten work and assignments.

In this article, we bring in parental perspectives and elaborate on their experiences to gain a deeper understanding of the issue. We research alongside Isha to consolidate teaching strategies and worksheets that can help her daughter focus on the overall neatness and presentation of her written work.

For ease of understanding, the article anchors to the pre-primary and primary grades. However, the strategies can be used by teachers and extrapolated to higher grades as well. Let’s read and learn more about the commonly observed mistakes in early learning.

Letter reversals

Also known as mirror writing, it is often confused with spelling mistakes. In the story above, Riti made a commonly occurring letter reversal between lowercase formations of the letters ‘b’ and ‘d’. Other frequently observed reversals are

  • lowercase ‘p’ and ‘q’
    • lowercase ‘m' and 'w'
    • lowercase 'n' and 'u'
    • lowercase 's' and 'z'

Reversals are observed in digits such as ‘6’ and ‘9’. They are also common between letters and digits like lowercase ‘e’ and the digit ‘3’, uppercase ‘L’ and ‘7’.

At the beginning of their learning journey, children move from picture recognition to letter recognition. Therefore, they perceive letters of the alphabet as images. For example, in the image below, their brain will comprehend both pictures as the moon, irrespective of how the image is oriented.

They may apply the same logic to letters of the alphabet. So, the letter ‘d’, written as ‘b’, is still perceived as ‘d’.

This causes young learners like Riti to confuse similar looking letters like ‘b’ and ‘d’. As learning progresses, they understand that each letter has a different sound, and sometimes, a letter flips its orientation to make a different sound. It is a natural part of development and children typically outgrow it with reading and writing practice.

The following strategies can be used by parents in their home environment. It is crucial to focus on one letter at a time to prevent confusion.

  • Use tactile material for writing: Provide various means to express written work. Look at the worksheet that Isha made for her daughter to spend time with the letter ‘d’ and internalize its lowercase formation.

Make a similar worksheet for the letter ‘b’. Apart from stickers, select from a range of readily available materials like clay, paint, crayons, sand, or even rangoli colours. Teach the correct stroke sequence through tracing and rainbow writing.

  • Games: Play games designed to sort commonly reversed letters. For example, place flashcards for the letters p and q in the appropriate box. You may also ask them to colour the letters to strengthen their recognition.

Create worksheets with intentional mistakes to make them hunt for letter reversals. It helps them be mindful of the errors in their written work.

Random capitalizations

As students begin to pick writing pace, they tend to focus on completing their work fast. They end up making mistakes not due to a lack of understanding, but due to a lack of focus. Randomly capitalizing letters between words and sentences is a common mistake.

Kopal Rastogi is an educator with over 17 years of tutoring experience. She also supports her 7-year-old daughter academically and observes, “Children often overlook small details because they tend to focus more on completing their tasks quickly rather than reviewing their work for accuracy.” The haste to complete tasks along with unclear capitalization rules creates messy outputs. Here are some strategies to help overcome this challenge.

  • Visual aids: Create posters or charts with basic capitalization rules. Mention the rules in a simple and legible way. State that uppercase letters are used at the beginning of the sentence, in the word ‘I’, and in all special naming words (proper nouns).
  • Read aloud: Read stories with simple sentences. Point out how capitals are used in the story. Encourage the child to question wherever they don’t understand the capitalization rule.
  • Model: Create sample written work in your own handwriting that is neat and uses the correct case of the letters. It helps children gain clarity about the writing expected from them.
  • Proofread: Once a writing task concludes, provide them with immediate feedback. If feedback comes too late, it will most likely be ignored as it is difficult to recall the circumstances under which the errors appeared.
  • Games: Similar to the hunting game in letter reversals, create worksheets with intentional mistakes. Such an exercise gives them a sense of achievement and allows them to focus on the specific details in their own work.

Inconsistent size, shape, and spacing

Kids as young as 3-8 years of age often write in illegible strokes. Such writing does not follow any pattern. You may observe that they

  • write same letters in different sizes within a given sentence
  • spill letters and words outside the margin
  • forget to dot the lowercase i and j or cross the lowercase t
  • forget to add space (or add too much space) between words
  • add random space between the letters of a word
  • reverse letters into meaningless formations

It is important to avoid diagnosing dysgraphia, just because the handwriting is illegible. Even though it is natural to be concerned, parents must understand that learning disorders are detected only after careful professional examination of the cognitive abilities and academic performance.

Unclear handwriting, with a mix of letter sizes and spacing, is a natural part of academic development. At times, it could simply be a product of haste. A lot of it resolves itself with more practise and proper guidance. Listed below are a few other effective strategies and tools for better presentation.

  • Provide time: Classroom lessons follow an average pace where slow writers may feel overwhelmed. They struggle to match their teacher’s (or peers’) speed and end up making mistakes.

This can be easily resolved in a home learning environment where children can set their own pace for writing. Parents and guardians must offer extra time, so that the learner does not feel pressurized to complete a task. It helps them write more mindfully while paying attention to the size, formation, and spacing details that they miss in their classrooms. “Consistency is fundamental – steady reinforcement through reading and revision gradually develops confidence and clarity in written work. Balancing my roles as a parent and an educator, I have learned that patient persistence pays off over time,” says Kopal.

  • Alternative writing tools: Provide a variety of writing tools such as pencils and crayons. As mentioned earlier, use tactile material like clay, paint, or sand for tracing and writing practise.

The margins may not be able to sufficiently support the child’s pencil strokes. Isha created a simple worksheet to provide more margin support. The coloured space helped her daughter write in consistent letter sizes and spacing.

Erroneous spellings and punctuations

One of the most widely occurring concerns with writing is recurring spelling and punctuation mistakes. Spelling mistakes are a complex problem and the solutions may not be straightforward. Memorizing spellings can only take the learning so far.

For children to gain confidence with spellings, they must be aware of the grade-wise list of words they must know how to spell. Such lists are easily available on the internet. Use the strategies below while focusing on one specific spelling/punctuation mistake at a time.

  • Visual aids: Spelling and punctuation errors mainly arise from a lack of knowledge of the rules. Create charts and posters that clarify such rules. These may include
  • Spelling rules for prefixes and suffixes: Understanding how to spell new words by adding prefixes like ‘un’ and ‘mis’ and suffixes such as ‘tion’ and ‘able’. If children know how to spell ‘fit’, they can derive the spelling for ‘misfit’, ‘unfit’ by adding prefixes. Similarly, they can use the spelling of ‘communicate’ to spell the words ‘communication’ and ‘communicable’.
  • Recognizing patterns: Certain spellings follow patterns. For example, words with double consonants like ‘fill’, ‘fall’, ‘still’, and ‘stall’, or rhyming words like ‘lamp’, ‘damp’, and ‘cramp’.
  • Rules for making plurals: Understanding rules for turning words into their plural forms like ‘leaf’ to ‘leaves’, and ‘knife’ to ‘knives’.
  • Rules for changing the verb form: Adding ‘ed’ or ‘ing’ to change the spellings of verbs. If they know how to spell ‘dance’, they can easily form the words ‘danced’ and ‘dancing’.
  • Commonly misspelled words: For easy reference, make lists of words they misspell often.
  • Reading backward: This strategy is helpful in hunting punctuation errors like commas, periods, question marks, and exclamation marks. Once the writing task is complete, ask the child to read the last sentence first and mark the punctuation errors. Next, ask them to read the second-to-last sentence, and so on. This helps them break the content flow and takes their mind away from the meaning of the sentence. This dissociation helps them focus specifically on the missing or misplaced punctuation marks.
  • Read along: Reading with kids is a widely recommended method to build language familiarity, expand vocabulary, and spell correctly. Storybooks, picture books, and comics are one of the most popular means to inculcate reading. But reading can be plugged in through alternative means too. Rashmi Guptha is a freelance design engineer. She ensures that her seven-year-old son reads regularly. She reflects, “We introduced compulsory reading depending on the energy level, interest, and mood. Either I read to him or we have a role play of dialogues from his favourite comics. I also introduced more games that had an instruction manual to read, so that the toy can be assembled before it is ready to be played with. We also subscribed to a kid’s newspaper just to encourage him to read and get familiar with the language as well as the world around.”
  • Daily writing: Maintaining journals can inculcate regular writing practise. It could be spellchecking, daily diary, a happy thought, or a weekly collection. “Daily writing is a must. Reflection on writing (going back and checking the work) is what I at least have him do at home,” says Chandrika.

Kopal has been making her daughter write consistently with great results. She reaffirms, “Regular 10-minute writing sessions have been very useful at home. Reading aloud together helps strengthen spelling awareness, while slow, deliberate writing improves letter formation and spacing. Encouraging my child to read each sentence before turning the page promotes self-checking and reflection.”

Parents’ corner

As the education landscape evolves, handwritten work is losing visibility and value. It is reflected in the widespread integration of digital mediums in several aspects of schooling such as assignments, assessments, marking, and communication. Yet, when a concerned parent observes mistakes with basic spellings, capitalization, and spacing in their child’s written work, the problem hits a little too deeply. Its weight is heavy and the stakes are high. In the story at the beginning of the article, Isha’s desperation to understand the issue, made her worry about her daughter’s learning abilities. Even though it is reasonable to get anxious, the only way forward is to read, research, talk, and support.

There are a few more steps that parents can take to help resolve recurring issues. Collaborate with the teacher and talk to her about the specific mistakes that you’ve observed. Ask for her inputs in differentiating between handwriting challenges and academic capabilities. Apart from written answers, use verbal, typed, or creative art forms as a medium of expression that will help build confidence.

It is also important to encourage children to review their own work. We read in the story that Isha’s daughter could correct the spelling mistake when she was prompted to take another look. Nevertheless, self-reviews are challenging with young learners as they may not see value in doing the same thing repetitively. Rashmi recalls her experience with her son and says, “I encouraged him to review but it often goes unseen… either because he is in a hurry to complete the work and get back to play or because he finds it boring to redo, especially mathematics. In my opinion, self-correction for kids of this age is a little challenging.”

Even though self-reviews are difficult to apply, they are a useful tool to achieve learning that lasts. Namrata Talwar Ohri is a mother, mind trainer, and happiness coach who works closely with schools. When asked if self-correction works, she says “It is always the self-correction that stays with the children. Not that the correction emphasized by the teachers and parents don’t stay but the learning and the self-confidence boosts up with self-correction. As an educator and a parent, we can always make them aware about the rightful ways to make them proficient in written work and speaking skills. But in the end, it has to be their willingness to receive the same. If a child takes it as a pressure and compulsion to study, it’s not going to help in the long run.”

As academics moves towards self-learning, parents need to be active partners in their child’s learning journey. Their presence, involvement, and collaboration with teachers goes a long way in helping children write neatly. For children to pay attention to small details of their written work, parents and teachers must guide with patience and persistence.

Surabhi Agarwal is a writer and instructional designer. She can be reached at surabhi.edtech@gmail.com.

The ripple effect

This article uses the skill of writing to address a child’s attention to detail. However, this discussion about attentiveness is not just an academic pursuit. Whether it’s a child or an adult, attentiveness (or the lack of it), spills over every aspect of our lives.

An artist’s focus on the quality and direction of their brush strokes can make a painting come alive. Just a dash of one ingredient can turn an ordinary dish into a culinary experience that takes the consumer down a memory lane. A musician’s attention to the fine notes can create a song that lives through generations. Focus plays a significant role in all creative pursuits such as art and craft, singing, playing music, dancing, cooking, theatre, and filmmaking.

Similarly, focus helps scientists look into minor details of their research that may otherwise get overlooked. It allows sportsmen to push their strengths and be aware of their opponents’ weaknesses. It helps journalists break news stories and nail interviews. Focus is what makes businessmen spot opportunities and politicians convert possibilities. On the battlefield, it draws the line between a win and a loss.

Childhood is a ripe time to create the foundation for focussed work. As parents and caregivers, it is crucial for us to differentiate between paying attention and chasing perfection. Asking children to focus on the small details of their routine activities does not mean that they will not make errors. Children should not work under the pressure of perfection as it may lead them into being afraid of making mistakes. The aim is to guide them towards mindful work, provide kind and constructive feedback, and give them tools to self-review their work.

When we look closely around us, paying attention to detail and thoughtfulness in our everyday work translates simple efforts into extraordinary piece of work. It is our duty to enable the next generation to understand that their attention is the currency on which they can build their lives.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Surabhi Agarwal
Attentiveness (or the lack of it) spills over every aspect of our lives. Surabhi Agarwal writes about how focus is crucial to help children and adults work mindfully and self-review, so as tomake mistakes and learn from them.

item.title: The devil is in the detail
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
I’ve been a writing teacher for the better part of three decades, and one of the things I repeat ad infinitum (and possibly, nauseum, for the listeners) is that they must (absolutely must) check their copy carefully before submitting. It’s the misused apostrophes and misplaced commas, the unnecessary capitals and the missing full stops, and of course, the run-on text without appropriate paragraphing that make me groan. I know in most cases it is not a lack of knowledge, but just a rush to be done with an assignment, to get past the task. These quibbles are seen as a mark of an obsessive mind, generally. I tell my students that the consequences of not paying attention to what they see as “tiny quibbles” will become evident when they apply for a job. If those recruiters are anything like me, they will immediately distrust the mind that missed those details.

A former boss dubbed it the “not quite right” factor – NQR, for short. We build beautiful edifices, with marble stairways and high ceilings, but in a corner somewhere will be a bit of fallen plaster, a chipped tile, or, most familiarly, a sign hung crookedly. We pay for certain clothing brands because of what we call “the finish” and what designers and creators call “the detailing”: smooth edges and perfectly folded hems, no loose threads anywhere. We appreciate the little touches in restaurants, hotels, and other service establishments that make us willing to pay just a bit more. We know, instinctively, that when the small things are attended to so painstakingly, the big things are likely to be well sorted out.

Of course, detail can sometimes be cosmetic, just a veneer that hides either shallowness or even absence of something substantive and real. But one can usually tell the difference between an attention to detail that comes from real depth and care, and that which is just decoration. When someone takes pride in their work, they tend to be uncompromising about the quality of their output – it has to be the best they can do. Writer Ann Patchett once said in an interview that she doesn’t stop working on a novel until she is satisfied that it is the best book she can write. Similarly, mathematicians aim for proofs that demonstrate “elegance” and “parsimony”, that is, explanations that are clean and clear, with nothing that is redundant.

One can find any number of examples from every field that emphasize the need for care and focus, and the cover theme of this month’s issue illustrates this by unpacking how to build such an attitude in children. If attention to detail, and the focus it demands, becomes a habit, then it is not just the veneer; it is something that runs through everything one does.
item.excerpt:encoded: I’ve been a writing teacher for the better part of three decades, and one of the things I repeat ad infinitum (and possibly, nauseum, for the listeners) is that they must (absolutely must) check their copy carefully before submitting.

item.title: Building bridges: cultivating peace in education
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ragini

"Peace cannot be kept by force; it can only be achieved by understanding." – Albert Einstein

Education is more than just the transfer of knowledge; it is the foundation upon which societies build their future. In today’s world, where conflict and division often dominate, the role of education in promoting peace has never been more crucial. As an art teacher, I have seen how creative expression can become a bridge between people, breaking down barriers and fostering understanding. Art provides students with a space to express their emotions, embrace different perspectives, and develop empathy – key elements in cultivating peace.

Schools are not just places where students learn facts; they are environments that shape their beliefs, attitudes, and values as well. Unfortunately, traditional education systems often focus on competition and individual success, neglecting the importance of emotional intelligence and interpersonal relationships. This can lead to stress, isolation, and even conflict among students. Instead, education should prioritize inclusivity, communication, and respect for diverse viewpoints. When students feel heard and valued, they develop a sense of belonging, which naturally contributes to a more peaceful learning environment.

Art has a unique ability to foster this sense of connection. Unlike spoken language, which can sometimes create misunderstandings, art allows individuals to express thoughts and emotions in ways that transcend cultural and linguistic differences. When students engage in artistic activities, they are not just creating something visually appealing; they are sharing a part of themselves. Whether through painting, sculpture, music, or theatre, artistic expression encourages them to explore their own identities while also appreciating the experiences of others.

One of the most profound aspects of art education is its ability to cultivate empathy. A few years ago, I worked with a group of students on an art project about global crises. One student, who had never met a refugee, painted a child fleeing a war with only a backpack and a teddy bear. Through this exercise, he began to ask deeper questions about displacement, loss, and resilience. His perspective changed, and he started advocating for inclusivity in his school, even organizing a fundraiser for displaced families. This is the power of art – it transforms understanding into action.

Art also serves as a medium for conflict resolution. Many young people struggle with expressing their emotions verbally, leading to misunderstandings or aggressive behaviour. I recall a case where two students had a long-standing rivalry, often resulting in classroom arguments. I paired them for a collaborative mural project where they had to depict “harmony in diversity.” Over time, as they discussed colours, themes, and techniques, the tension between them eased. By the end, they were not only proud of their work but had also developed a mutual respect. Art had given them a non-verbal way to build a relationship.

Beyond individual expression, collaborative art projects further reinforce the values of teamwork, patience, and compromise. When students work together on a mural or group performance, they learn to listen to one another, respect different ideas, and find common ground. These are essential skills not only for artistic creation but also for maintaining harmony in any social setting. In situations where conflicts arise, collaborative art can act as a bridge, bringing together students who may have had disagreements and encouraging them to find solutions through shared creativity.

Cultural exchange is another way art contributes to peace in education. Exposure to diverse artistic traditions broadens students' understanding of the world, reducing biases and promoting appreciation for different cultures. I once introduced a project where students had to create artworks inspired by a culture different from their own. A student, hesitant at first, chose to study Japanese ukiyo-e prints. By the end of the project, he was fascinated by the philosophy behind the art and even began practicing calligraphy. His newfound appreciation led him to explore more cultures, making him more open-minded in everyday interactions.

Art also plays a crucial role in emotional healing and resilience. Many students face personal challenges, including family struggles, academic pressure, or social anxieties. I once had a student who rarely spoke in class but found solace in sketching. Over time, she began to share her drawings, which depicted her emotions vividly. One day, she presented a piece showing a storm clearing into a sunrise. When asked about it, she simply said, “This is how I feel now.” Art became a turning point in her confidence, proving how creativity can be a tool for self-discovery and inner peace.

To cultivate peace through education, schools must actively integrate artistic expression into their curriculum. Peace-themed art projects, such as creating murals that symbolize unity or designing posters advocating nonviolence, can inspire students to think about their role in building a better world. Additionally, storytelling through art – whether in the form of comics, animation, or theatre – allows students to explore social issues in a creative and engaging manner. Interdisciplinary approaches, where art is combined with subjects like history, literature, or social studies, can further deepen students' understanding of global challenges and encourage thoughtful discussions.

Beyond the classroom, community engagement is essential in reinforcing the message of peace. Organizing public exhibitions, street art festivals, or art-based social initiatives can bring together students, teachers, and families, creating a sense of collective responsibility for fostering harmony. Schools can also collaborate with local artists, cultural organizations, and international programs to provide students with broader perspectives on peace building.

However, the most important factor in cultivating peace in education is the role of educators themselves. Teachers must lead by example, demonstrating kindness, patience, and respect in their daily interactions. A peaceful classroom begins with a peaceful teacher. I once had a mentor who never raised her voice, even in the most chaotic moments. Instead, she used calmness and art-based reflection exercises to guide students toward resolution. The impact of her approach was evident – students felt safe, heard, and understood, making conflicts rare and short-lived.

Education has the power to shape not only individuals but entire societies. By embracing art as a means of connection, empathy, and emotional expression, we can transform our classrooms into spaces of understanding and cooperation. In a world divided by differences, art serves as a universal bridge, reminding us that despite our unique experiences, we are all part of the same human race. Through creativity, education can become a force for peace – one brushstroke, one story, one shared vision at a time.

Dr. Ragini is Academic Head, Sunbeam, Lahartara, Varanasi. She can be reached at raginisunbeamlht@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ragini
Can peace be taught? Ragini shares how schools, especially art classrooms, can nurture understanding and harmony.

item.title: The courage to disagree
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

BOOM! There was a loud explosion from the ground floor.

The new principal (just two weeks into her job) of Sri Kumaran Children’s Home, (Bengaluru) Nandashree Natarajan, hurried out of her office (on the same floor) towards the location of the explosion.

Expecting to see teachers crowding in the corridors, she braced herself to remain calm, even as she raced. To her astonishment, the corridors were empty. She was joined by just one other teacher, who had left his class on the third floor to investigate the noise.

The chemistry laboratory assistant and her helpers were safe but shaken, after they had (in their ignorance) poured water over mixed residual chemicals in the disposal area, resulting in a violent reaction.

Walking back to her office, the principal was relieved – Thank God it was a minor accident and nobody was hurt in the process!

But why had no other teacher come out to see what was wrong? It was too loud a sound not to be heard on all floors of the building. Something was holding them back – what was it?

Slowly, she began to see that the accident had nailed the burning issue: most of the faculty were in a compliant mode, doing their work sincerely and exactly as they were told to do. It was their job, their workplace, and they strove to meet expectations. As they went about their daily chores, there was a palpable lack of a sense of belonging or even a smile. Had they been told to leave their classes to investigate the cause of the explosion, they would have dutifully done so. They were busy covering the syllabus and teaching their classes as always: and doing it excellently.

The principal yearned to bring in a more open culture: where there was a sense of camaraderie among the teachers and herself, where opinions were fearlessly voiced and everyone felt that this was THEIR school.

How was she to set about triggering that change? She decided to turn this into an Action Research (AR) Problem.

In order to frame the problem, she roped in a few teachers who she had already identified as being more communicative. With their help, she systematically surveyed the entire faculty’s needs: and the findings were startling. Almost all of them articulated the need for free and open communication!

So, the AR problem became: To cultivate a work environment grounded in mutual respect, open communication, and shared values.

Next, her team designed a set of activities for five days of empowerment during the Teachers’ Correction-Time (3 to 4 pm): Motivational Monday, Trust Tuesday, Whale Done Wednesday, Thought-Provoking Thursday, and Festive Friday.

On the first Monday, this team held a motivational session to draw out teachers’ gratitude towards any colleague who had helped them in the past. For the first time, teachers openly articulated appreciation of their colleagues – which many found very moving. On Trust Tuesday, sharing of personal stories between random pairs of teachers was intended to build openness and trust.

It was after this that the penny dropped. For, just before Whale Done Wednesday, teachers expressed their reluctance to participate further – citing workload and time crunch.

The principal paused: could resistance turn into collaboration? Instead of confronting her teachers, she solicited their suggestions and feedback. This was the turning point in her AR: for excellent suggestions emerged, e.g. discussion of real-life case studies. Her team immediately crafted meaningful case studies that led to rich discussions on the following Wednesday.

Now that the ball had been set rolling, Thought-Provoking Thursday didn’t need much designing by the core team, as a Grade I teacher volunteered to make a presentation! It all culminated in a truly Festive Friday where visibly smiling teachers showcased their talents.

Happy ending? NO! “This is just the beginning of an ongoing effort to sustain bonds between my teachers and to keep communication alive,” admitted Nandashree. “While I can see less stiffness and far more smiles, I know that I have to work towards being part of a team, rather than an isolated head of a school.”

That ‘setback’ on Tuesday was in fact an advancement: for teachers who had heretofore been quietly compliant now trusted their principal enough to openly disagree!

So here is a touchstone for heads of schools/teachers:

  • How often do your teachers openly disagree with you?
  • How freely do your students express opposing views?

References

1. Whale DoneThe Power of Positive Relationships By Ken Blanchard

2. https://www.facebook.com/reel/1351022283229308

Neeraja Raghavan is Founder-Director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. She can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
When no teacher or student steps out of class after an accident, the principal is forced to look within. Neeraja Raghavan writes about what it really takes to build an open school culture.

item.title: Devolution of power and special rights
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: In our previous conversation, we were discussing local self-government and the need for us to create formal structures for that, but there is risk of provincialism and localism if we establish a high level of autonomy for local self-governments. You said there was more to decentralization than this.

Anubhuti: Yes, I read some constitutional debates on this issue. It is more complex than I thought it was. There was significant debate on the 5th and 6th Schedule in the Constitution.

Ira: What are these Schedules in the Constitution?

Anubhuti: There are 12 Schedules in our constitution. Schedules are like addenda that specify details and in some cases exceptions that are needed to complete the Articles in the body of the Constitution. For instance, Schedules contain the list of states and union territories, salaries of important roles like the president, governors, and judges, the wordings of various oaths, union, state, and concurrent lists, the list of recognized languages in India, and so on.

Ira: So,the 5th and 6th Schedules contain exceptions made for some regions.

Anubhuti: Yes. These Schedules mainly focus on special provisions for administration and control for communities of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in particular geographies in different states. The 5th Schedule is for all such regions apart from Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram, and the 6th Schedule is for these four states.

Ira: And what kind of rights do these regions have that other parts of the nation do not?

Anubhuti: In principle, these communities were recognized as having a long history that is very different from the rest of the people and cultures that comprise India. Primarily, the Constitution allows self-determination or sovereignty in a different form to these regions.

These four states have additional governance structures in the form of regional and state level councils that are reserved for the people of these communities. They have the right to govern themselves, even in terms of making laws related to both civil and criminal justice, which will not be regulated by laws made by the Legislature. They are accountable to the president, in that only the president has the right to make any changes to the definitions of these geographies.

This led to a prolonged debate in the Constitutional Assembly. The objections to these provisions came from Kuladhar Chaliha, Brajeshwar Prasad, Gopinath Bardoloi, Rohini Kumar Chaudhuri, and Shibban Lal Saxena[*]. Over all, the objections were of three kinds – 1) that all regions defined as India needed to be integrated into the main country rather than allow the possibility of separatism, (2) Many tribes were “very primitive and simple people and they have not forgotten their old ways of doing summary justice when they have a grievance against anyone. If you allow them to rule us or run the administration it will be a negation of justice or administration and it will be something like anarchy,”* (Chaliha, Kulidhar) and they had norms and rituals that were not in sync with the way the Constitution imagined India, and (3) the Constitution said the parliament could not impose any laws on these regions without their consent, which seemed contradictory to the spirit of constitutionalism.

In relation to point 3, it was suggested that the number of representatives should be very small, in proportion to the population of the region. Shibban Lal Saxena recommended that autonomy be given, but with a timeline by which the tribal regions could be assimilated into the nation.

Ira: But if they were becoming part of India, why not insist that they accept common law? Our constitution is essentially democratic in terms of laws; and if we believe in democracy, we will find the control of common law convincing.

Anubhuti: This is the tension between indigenous culture, which one has lived for generations, and a common law, which would amount to an imposition on those cultures – something the indigenous communities did not want. Rohini Kumar Chaudhuri pointed out that many sections of the tribes had demonstrated acceptance of common culture and law and that it was only a matter of time before everyone would accept the common law.

But in response to the demand for assimilation into mainland culture, a member of the Constituent Assembly, Nichols-Roy said, “But what is culture? Does it mean dress or eating and drinking. If it means eating and drinking or ways of living, the hill tribes can claim that they have a better system than some of the people of the plains. I think the latter must rise up to their standard. Among the tribesmen, there is no difference between class and class. Even the Rajas and Chiefs work in the fields together with their labourers. They eat together. Is that practised in the plains? The whole of India has not reached that level of equality. …They had their own rule, their own language, court, and culture. To say that the culture of these people must be swallowed by another culture, unless it is a better culture and unless it be by a process of gradual evolution, is rather very surprising to anyone who wants to build up India as a nation and bring all people together. … India should rise to that feeling or idea of equality and real democracy which the tribal people have. They should not for a second think that these people should give up their democracy and equality and be swallowed up by another culture which is quite different from what they have been used to, and which is considered by them not at all suitable to their Society.” (Nichols-Roy, J. J. M.)

The basic point Nichols-Roy raised was that others were essentializing the tribes as being primitive and not modern or educated enough to be democratic. They had many traditions and practices that were more democratic than the rest of India practised. He was making the argument that multiculturalists make today.

Ira: What do multiculturalists say?

Anubhuti: Multiculturalists claim that democracy has become too individualistic. They say, “Individual identities are formed in a cultural context, and ignoring or devaluing the culture is discriminatory both to the individual and to the group. Thus, a commitment to the equal worth of all individuals means equal respect to their culture and equal treatment of all groups.” (Srinivasan, Janaki). Quoting Will Kymlicka, Janaki Srinivasan says, “Multicultural societies should provide self-governance rights for indigenous peoples and cultural rights for ethnic groups.[†]

Ira: So, in their debates, the Constituent Assembly had already spoken of the kind of democracy that people are still arguing about, 75 years later.

Anubhuti: The 5th and 6th Schedules speak to self-governance rights of indigenous peoples. The details are articulated in Article 244 in Part X, and Article 371 in Part XXI of the Constitution.

Ira: Last time, you said that this reminded you of minority rights. How are the two related?

Anubhuti: This basic multiculturalist logic is also adopted when it comes to the protection of rights for minority cultures. Our constitution allows protection of languages and culture for linguistic and religious minorities. This is articulated as Cultural and Educational Rights in Articles 29 and 30. The logic is the same, in the sense that we have numerous cultures, religions, and languages in India, and individuals and groups carry their identities in this form. So, they should have the right to protect these cultures, for which, obviously, they need educational rights.

Ira: But why minorities only? We will not be able to identify some groups as minorities, for instance, Hindus.

Anubhuti: The Constitution does not define minorities at all. In any place, people speaking a language different from the local language would be a minority. For instance, my parents are Tamil and they moved to Maharashtra. Many Tamil friends of mine studied in schools run by the South Indian Association, where they learnt Tamil, Hindi, and English. I went to a local private school, where I had to learn Marathi, Hindi, and English.

Ira: And there are many schools that adopt one religion or the other and teach them too. For instance, convent schools have Christian practices, Vedic schools have Hindu, Madrasas have Arabic and the Koran, Jain schools adopt practices from Jainism, and so on. But they all follow the common curriculum with all other subjects.

Anubhuti: Exactly. They are claiming the rights given to us by the Constitution. I find Article 28 more interesting in helping claim these rights without infringing on the rights of other religions. According to this article, state funded schools cannot include any religious instruction. Private schools can provide religious instruction but not enforce them on children of other religions.

It makes a clear distinction between religious instruction and rituals/practices that are religious worship. No student is required to participate in either, and in case of minors, the consent of parents or guardians is needed if the school wants them to participate in either instruction or rituals.

Ira: Isn’t this same principle applied in family law also?

Anubhuti: Yes. We have different laws for marriage and inheritance coming from different religions. Marriages are registered under a particular religious law. If someone does not want to attach themselves to a religion, they can register their marriage under the Special Marriages Act.

It is funny though that most people understand the Special Marriages Act as limited only to inter-religious marriages. You see, religions are so deeply embedded in our psyche that it is difficult to separate our identities from the religion ascribed to us – by birth.

Ira: Sigh! It is not only our gender, but also our religion that we carry from birth. I wouldn’t call it a problem though. There is nothing wrong with being religious.

Anubhuti: Of course there isn’t. The Constitution clearly says so. The only problem is, most codified religions have gender discrimination embedded within them. That is why feminists have a problem with the multiculturalist recommendations of protecting minority cultures. This implies status quo insofar as gender discrimination is concerned. There is this perpetual tension between fundamental rights and group rights – at least with respect to culture and religious rights.

Ira: Which is why Nichols-Roy was arguing that we should not limit our understanding of tribes and that many of them have practiced gender and class equality for generations – and so in a way they have been more democratic than others.

Anubhuti: True. This is called the problem of essentialism. We cannot and should not essentialize anyone as being one or the other. That speaks for our personal identities too. Which brings us to the next topic – Citizenship, Rights, and Duties. That discussion will be as complex as the one on cultures, if not more.

[*] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/06-sep-1949/#117852

[†] Srinivasan, Janaki. 2008. “Democracy” in “Political Theory – An Introduction” edited by Bhargava, Rajeev and Acharya, Ashok.

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
What do the 5th and 6th Schedules reveal about indigenous rights, multiculturalism, and the idea of equality? In Prakash Iyer’s piece, Ira and Anubhuti continue their discussion on the rights of minorities and the meaning of democracy.

item.title: Brewing different futures in the tea gardens of Assam
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Gautam Bisht

India is the second-largest tea producer in the world, with the state of Assam contributing nearly half of its total output. Tea plantations in Assam were first established by the British in 1839 in an effort to break China’s monopoly in the global tea trade. When local labour proved insufficient, the British brought in tribal communities from across India, binding them into a brutal system of bonded labour. Today, the descendants of those workers remain trapped in the legacy of this exploitative system, facing persistent poverty, social exclusion, and systemic neglect.

Popular perception in India often romanticizes tea gardens as serene and picturesque landscapes. In reality, research has documented alarming levels of poor health outcomes (Boro & Saxena, 2022) and low educational attainment (Laskar, 2024) within tea plantation communities. As a response, our team at the Havey Institute for Global Health at Northwestern Medicine, led by Dr Jyothi Puthumana and a local non-profit organization, the Centre for Development Initiatives (CDI), designed a project that seeks to create new possibilities for the community by supporting their health and educational outcomes. This article focuses on the educational framework of our work.

The vast majority of children drop out of school before reaching high school, cutting short their chances of pursuing alternative livelihoods. With limited options, many end up working in the same tea gardens as their parents. As Sr. Lisa Elavunkal, Director of CDI, puts it, “Preventing school dropout is critical to breaking this cycle of intergenerational poverty and hardship.” Evidence suggests that strengthening foundational skills in mathematics and reading among young children leads to improved school continuation rates (Kumar et al., 2023), guiding our decision to work with younger children (4-12 year olds), and designing our educational approach around three core features.

Feature one: What is to be taught?

While poor foundational learning is a widespread issue across India, tea plantation communities are uniquely and disproportionately affected. According to the most recent national assessment, 61% of class 7 students in Assam can read a class 2 text. In contrast, our baseline assessment of 500 students from tea plantation areas found that only 30% of class 7 students could do the same. This alarming gap highlights the urgent need for targeted intervention. To address this, we adopt the internationally recognized Teaching at the Right Level (TaRL) approach, which focuses on helping children master basic skills through level-based group instruction. By prioritizing foundational learning, we aim to ensure that children not only stay in school but also gain the tools to succeed at it.

Feature two: Who does it?

To address teacher shortages and irregular school instruction, we established community learning centers led by local youth from the tea gardens. These young mentors are part of the All Adivasi Student Association of Assam, a community organization that has long championed the rights of plantation workers. Ten local youth, aged 18-25, engage directly with around 500 children across 20 villages on the foundational skills mentioned above. We equip these mentors through ongoing training that helps them grow both as educators and as individuals, enabling them to design learning activities and track learner progress. Many of these mentors are pursuing college degrees and aspire to become government school teachers, open private educational centers, or launch community-based enterprises. Their leadership within the villages not only supports children’s learning today, but also offers inspiring examples of what’s possible for the next generation.

Feature three: How to do it?

The third core feature of our educational programme is to build on students’ local cultures and languages to develop the foundational skills they need for school. Most tea garden students do not speak the languages used in schools, such as Assamese or English. Instead, within their families, they speak tribal languages like Sadri, Oraon, or Mundari. During tribal festivals like Karma, community elders engage with students through stories and songs, helping them develop a strong sense of their own linguistic abilities. Similarly, children’s popular games and their participation in household activities such as tea leaf collection help them develop implicit mathematical reasoning. Our programme emphasizes connecting what children already know from their cultural and daily experiences with what they are expected to learn in school. Teachers, once prompted during a training, enjoyed designing activities for reading and mathematics based on children’s everyday lives. (See some examples below.)

Challenges and possibilities

Development initiatives are often caught between two worlds: the long-term vision of a dignified life, nurtured in partnership with marginalized communities, and the short-term, measurable representation of that vision demanded by funders. These two are not always aligned, and sometimes the difference plays out in painful ways. Community members are asked to spend hours filling out forms to demonstrate "impact," offering data that fits neatly into regression analyses. They are asked to pose in front of cameras – cast both as props of poverty and as agents of change – so that somewhere, a well-meaning donor is convinced. We tread carefully, aware that without deep reflection, development work can unintentionally reproduce the very hierarchies and harms it seeks to dismantle.

At the same time, it is within these tensions that new possibilities emerge. While this initiative is still in its early stages, it is trying to work out a model of how community-rooted mentorship, culturally responsive pedagogy, and a focus on foundational skills can meaningfully address educational inequities. We envision three pathways forward. First, by partnering more closely with government schools, elements of our pedagogy and contextualized learning materials can be integrated into the formal system. Second, the community learning centers themselves have the potential to evolve into self-sustaining entities through local fundraising, integration with existing youth organizations, or small tuition-based models for supplementary education. Third, in collaboration with local artists, we are developing digital multimedia materials rooted in the cultural contexts of tea garden communities. Rather than focusing on foundational skills, these materials aim to evoke themes of children’s agency, creativity, and transformation – bridging entertainment and inspiration. They can be accessed on mobile phones, extending their reach beyond the community learning centers and the duration of the project.

References

Boro, B. C., & Saxena, K. (2022). The impact of work-life balance on the wellbeing of employees in the tea gardens of Bodoland territorial region. International Journal of Health Sciences, 6(S3), Article S3. https://doi.org/10.53730/ijhs.v6nS3.9221

Kumar, D., Pratap, B., & Aggarwal, A. (2023). Children’s early foundational skills and education continuation in India: Heterogeneous analysis by caste, gender and location. World Development Perspectives, 30, 100502. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wdp.2023.100502

Laskar, R. (2024). Problems and Prospects of the Children in Tea Garden Areas of Assam: A Study on Kondoli Tea Estate. In Polity, Economy and Culture of North-East India. RUDRA PUBLISHERS & DISTRIBUTORS. https://www.researchgate.net/publication/387507407_POLITY_ECONOMY_AND_CULTURE_OF_NORTH-EAST_INDIA

Gautam Bisht is a PhD Candidate at Learning Sciences, Northwestern University, Illinois. He can be reached at gautambisht2025@u.northwestern.edu.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Gautam Bisht
Did you know that a cup of chai carries a long history of displacement and inequality? Gautam Bisht explores an education model for the children of India’s tea garden workers.

item.title: Rangoli reimagined
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Alekhya Bellapu

Every year at AIMEE International School, Sankranti – India’s vibrant harvest festival – is celebrated with unmatched enthusiasm. Over the years, our celebrations have grown into a grand, immersive experience: from bullock cart rides, Bhogi fires, cow worship, traditional cooking, and kite flying to festive rituals and joyful games. The whole school transforms into a space that radiates the season's joy.

Having led and celebrated this festival for three consecutive years, I wondered: what's next? As a cultural enthusiast overseeing cultural events, I reflected deeply. Festivals often reflect beautiful traditions, but they sometimes carry unspoken stereotypes too. One, particularly, caught my attention: that rangoli, the colourful floor art drawn during Sankranti, is “a woman’s job.” Women are expected to decorate the entrance, make pongal, and prepare for the festivities, while men bring home the harvest. I firmly believe that in ancient times, this practice did not evolve out of stereotype; rather, the work was shared in accordance to their ease of working together.

This year, I decided to shift this narrative.

Rangoli, to me, is more than mere decoration – it’s art. So, I asked myself: What if we reimagine rangoli as a collaborative expression of creativity and diversity? What if it became a way to include everyone, regardless of gender, age, or background, and reflect the true spirit of Sankranti?

I shared the idea with our school director – a rangoli competition where students and parents work together with the theme “Diversity in Sankranti.” For our youngest learners in pre-primary, it would be a chance to explore fine and gross motor skills through art. Parents of pre-primary children were invited to participate alongside their kids.
