To support them, we assigned students from grades 6 to 8 – not only to help but also to learn, collaborate, and celebrate as a team.

When I first floated the idea, a small worry lingered: Would anyone show up? But I remained hopeful. To my surprise, the registrations started pouring in – first, 3, then 10, and finally, 20 parents eagerly signed up.

And then, something magical happened.

On the day of the event, our school grounds came alive – not just with colour, but with shared energy. Fathers turned up, sleeves rolled up, working alongside their children. Parents from diverse religious and linguistic backgrounds came together, showcasing the festival’s inclusive spirit. It was, in every sense, overwhelming – and deeply heartwarming.

Each parent group was teamed up with students from higher grades. I had intentionally mixed groups to include both boys and girls. And one conversation stayed with me. A boy looked uncertain and said, “Ma’am, I don’t want to do this. Rangoli is a girl’s activity.”

“Why do you think so?” I asked.

He replied, “Because I’ve only seen my mom do it at home.”

“So,” I asked gently, “should only girls make art?”

He paused. “I like drawing and painting,” he said. “But not this.”

“Just give it a try,” I encouraged him. “See if you still feel the same after you start doing it.”

An hour later, he walked up to me, eyes shining. “Ma’am, I was wrong. To fill in colours is so satisfying. Drawing all those geometric patterns was really good. I’m sorry I said it was only for girls.”

That moment stayed with me – a slight shift in perspective, but a powerful one.

Across the campus, boys who had once been hesitant were now immersed in creating. Fathers helped mothers with intricate patterns. Laughter, conversation, and colour flowed freely. It wasn’t just a rangoli competition anymore – it was a celebration of creativity, teamwork, and breaking barriers. Last but not the least, our tiny tots created masterpieces in unison with their parents and the seniors in school, which showcased their patience, helped them improve their gross motor and fine motor skills. They also experienced immense joy in creating beautiful art works in collaboration.

The themes in the rangolis were breathtaking – capturing harvest scenes, kites soaring in the sky, bullocks, sugarcane, and most beautifully, the diversity of the festival. We honoured everyone’s efforts, celebrated the winners in a grand ceremony, and cherished the shared spirit that filled the air.

At the end of the day, I felt a deep sense of contentment. The event had come alive just the way I had hoped – not only as a celebration but as a quiet revolution against stereotypes.

One of my favourite moments came when my friends who are also my colleagues said to me, “Today felt different. It wasn’t just a celebration – it was a reflection.”

Indeed, it was.

Sankranti at AIMEE wasn’t just about tradition this year – it was about transformation. It was about giving every child, every parent, every voice a place in the celebration. It was about showing that art belongs to everyone – and so also joy.

As the colours settled and the last of the rangolis faded, one thing remained bright: the memory of a day when boundaries dissolved and creativity united us all.

Alekhya Bellapu is an academic leader and curious educator who enjoys watching questions unfold into discoveries. With a background in both CBSE and Cambridge curricula, she believes in hands-on, inquiry-led learning, where every moment sparks wonder, every conversation builds perspective, and every child becomes a seeker of the truth. She can be reached at alekhya.bellapu@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Alekhya Bellapu
At Sankranti celebrations in one school, Alekhya Bellapu shares how the simple act of rangoli decorations nurtured creativity and opened up conversations about art and gender.

item.title: When classrooms turn students into explorers!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

I loved vacations as a child. Vacations allowed us to do something different from the usual school life that always came with a strict timetable and a ticking clock. Vacations were like very long extended weekends where we could do what we wanted. But what was most exciting during vacations was if there was a ‘trip’. It was a thing – children had to be engaged differently during vacations, and it was best if it involved a ‘trip to somewhere’.

Sometimes it was as ordinary as visiting a part of the city that was far from where we lived. For many, visiting their hometown was the norm. For some, it meant going for a holiday to another city or even another country! But what was common between all these endeavours was the idea of travel.

As we return from the holiday season, I think about how ‘travel’ can become an opportunity to engage with the world and how we can bring attention to deepening this experience of meaningful engagement. Travel may sound like a luxury of the privileged, but why should we allow the capitalist view to dictate how we define and ‘consume’ travel. A train ride to one’s hometown or even just to the other end of the city, or a ride on the new metro can be seen as travel. In an age, where travel has become primarily about taking selfies for social media, where the name of the destination defines whether it is even worthy of sharing, it is perhaps worth assessing how our own relationship with travel has shifted. With staring into screens winning over gazing out of windows, and virtual chat rooms replacing conversations with travel companions, could this be an opportunity for education to step in and offer a new lens to look through?

This is why I love educators. We identify a problem, a disequilibrium of sorts and then take it very seriously because we know that here's an opportunity to bring in a new lens, a new perspective, a new pathway leading to several rabbit holes to jump into. And what can be most exciting is that we do this with a bunch of young minds who perhaps have larger capacities to see the world in new ways! This is when education becomes a process of engaging with the world, where learning is a given, not a measurable outcome.

If we are talking about travel as a means to engage with the world, it is imperative that I write about the brilliant Canadian author, illustrator, and conceptual artist, Keri Smith and her wonderful book How to be an Explorer of the World. The title says it all, doesn’t it! This is a book of 59 ideas of how to engage with your surroundings in novel ways. Take a look at the list below, which Smith scribbled in the middle of a sleepless night, which lead to the idea of this book.

Imagine stepping out of your house to buy onions, with just a few of these 13 lenses this list provides. The little walk down your street and back will transform your experience of it. Buying onions will no longer be the chore it possibly is right now! Can you imagine what will occur when we provide such tools to young minds (while also getting them to go buy the onions next time!) We may lose a few children to the world of curiosities (and no guarantee that you’ll get your onions!) When such tools become a lens, a way of seeing, the world opens up in expansive ways that allows all individuals, not only children, to stay connected to their surroundings, to life and to their sense of presence – which we are losing rapidly trying to catch up with the ‘smart world’. The author puts it delightfully in her preface when she says, “the real learning…is to be had in the midst of living!”

Travel can be skillfully extrapolated to the idea of new sights, tastes, rituals, languages, and cultural nuances through photography, travel magazines, travel and food shows, documentary films, and even audio podcasts that hold possibilities of exposure that a classroom setup cannot otherwise offer. I’ve seen a geography teacher artfully use an episode of a travel and food show exploring Kerala to introduce a unit on coastal plains. The conversations and curiosities with which the students entered this unit allowed a richness to the topic that simply teaching out of a textbook or PowerPoint presentation would never enable. Many years ago, I was introduced to a television series titled Raja, Rasoi Aur Anya Kahaniya by a history teacher who used it while exploring the Mauryan Empire. Her students were asked to watch a particular episode as homework and pick out information they got from the episode to add to their understanding of that period and its influences in present day India. When watching TV becomes homework, we have successfully made a shift in the narrative of what learning can look like!

I’d like to go back to the idea of travel, of actually going from one place to another – not necessarily from a familiar to an unfamiliar place, but simply journeying with intentionality. Private schools organize educational trips for their students with similar objectives, to create shared learning experiences, but these are not always affordable by all and has the potential to exclude. The fact remains that everyone travels, to go somewhere, near or far, new or old, and education can make this experience more meaningful in various ways. I remember reading the poem A Railway Carriage by Robert Louis Stevenson with class 4 students, when I set them off to write their own little poems of what they remember seeing outside a train window while travelling to some place. Most children who visited their hometown during the vacation had much to write about, while the city-bound children wrote vividly about observations within the city that daily train commuters would never even have noticed!

Equipping our little explorers with simple tools like the ones Smith suggests in her book, we educators can cleverly weave this idea of ‘seeing’ into the curriculum. Instead of giving math sums and essay writing as ‘holiday homework’, can we get children to walk around with journals, pens, and empty pockets collecting, drawing, searching, surveying, observing, asking, questioning, and in turn encouraging them to engage with their world in novel ways?

As educators, a big responsibility is how we honour what they bring back to the class in ways that are embedded in curiosity and respect. How can we ensure that the stories being shared aren’t a display of what their social status can access but about how they engaged with the world they set out to explore? I can envision large displays across a classroom wall that maps the various experiences of a group of children who perhaps explored languages and names of things and people on one such vacation or another exploration of patterns that everyone decided to explore.

In a class of 30-40 students, where each inhabits a world that is starkly different from the other, it is indeed unfortunate that we often forget the diversity that a classroom offers us. The nature of such explorations allows for meaning making that is embedded in one’s own context, own experience and in a lived reality that is local. With the obsession to make education “global”, knowledge building and learning are becoming homogenous in our classrooms, just like our cities, our food, our language and our capacities to see. It is essential that we do not forget the significance of indigenous systems of knowledge, local ecologies and intelligences, our truths and our contexts, and its absolute importance in learning and understanding the world at large. And the first step towards that could be to travel through it all like an explorer with a keen eye, a pen and journal, and large empty pockets!

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD in teacher education. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Beyond the old debate about travel vs tourism, Timira shares how educators can transform holiday homework into immersive and exploratory activities.

item.title: Performing mathematics
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Kavita Desai

Children are often not very fond of mathematics as they feel that the subject has no applicability in real life. Taking this into account, CBSE has made it mandatory for all teachers to do a competency based education (CBE) course, which encourages adopting a teaching approach that emphasizes real-world application of mathematical knowledge and skills.

Here, I would like to share my experience of conducting activity based lessons.

We were in the math lab and one of my students was attracted to a beautiful painting hanging on the wall. Noting his interest, and in the hope of using that to teach him math, I asked him to find the area of the painting. His immediate response was that this wouldn’t be possible to calculate that. This led me to wonder why children give up immediately and why they are not eager or curious to learn. It also gave me the idea of integrating the concept of areas with canvas paintings.

Competency based education with integration of art in mathematics

The activity was planned in such a way as to enhance student creativity, imagination, and visualization. The students were asked to combine different shapes like sector, quadrant, semicircle, circle, segment, triangle, square, rectangle, etc., and create their own paintings. Their paintings had to reflect their own likes, interests, and dreams.

Once they were ready with their paintings, I helped the students calculate the area of each shape and then add the numbers to find the area of their painting. When I conducted this activity, it increased students’ engagement in class. They were also happy that they got to take their beautiful creations back home.

If your students are not into paintings, you could perhaps ask them to make or design different objects from a set of given shapes to find areas of.

My students thoroughly enjoyed the following activity as well. From students, I saw them turn into engineers and designers who crafted cars, robots, cranes, and even buildings. Once they were ready, the students had to present their designs in front of the class, describe the process, the shapes they used to make their objects, and how they found the areas of those objects. The presentations could be in the form of a song, dance, or act. Here, I realized that there were poets and writers too in my class. And the best part of this activity was that my students reached their destination (finding the area of something) all on their own.

During this activity, the class was noisy, yes, but it was constructive noise. Noise that showcased the hidden talent in my students. And when taught like this, math classes will transform from abstract learning into creative and constructive learning.

My students’ positive response to the visual art and shape construction activities encouraged me to integrate performing arts into the learning of mathematics. Children can perform a dance showcasing different mathematical concepts. Bharatnatyam is a wonderful medium to do it. Different adivasi dance forms can showcase pyramids, the formation of hyperbola, parabola, etc. Through the hip hop dance form different angles can be formed. Mirror dance can give children a clear concept of the mirror image.

My math classes are now artistic. When will you begin performing mathematics?

Kavita Desai is a mathematics teacher at Vrajbhoomi International School, Anand. She can be reached at desaikavita818@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Kavita Desai
Can painting, dance, and performance help students find joy in mathematics? Kavita Desai shares an experiment that turned abstract math into creative, lived experience.

item.title: Making story-based learning real
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Deeptha Vivekanand

I still remember the first time my grade 9 physics teacher began a lesson on Newton’s laws with a folktale about a stubborn potter, his donkey, and three heavy pots. My state of inertia had been altered; the analogy landed, and my head buzzed with questions. Moments like these are why I plunged into advocating for storytelling in education 16 years ago. That lesson went on to illustrate all three of Newton’s laws – vividly and memorably. Presently, as a story-educator, I was able to turn that experience into a full lesson plan, with help from a colleague (included as an annexure). Looking back, it’s no surprise that I remember that class so clearly: stories stick. It also made me reflect on something bigger: why such lessons are rare, and how our schools badly need more of them.

I often see the same spark I once felt, reflected in students, when they are engaged in a lesson through a story. During a grade 9 unit on forces and free fall, we used story cards tracing Galileo’s observations and Newton’s insights. In the post-session feedback, one student remarked, “We started questioning everything and became genuinely interested in the theories of free fall.” This moment made it clear that when learners see concepts unfold as part of a story, they remember not just the 'what', but the 'why' and the 'who' – and most importantly, they stay curious.

In my role as Storytelling & Reading Lead at a CBSE school in Chandigarh, I’ve seen the energy storytelling unlocks – for both students and teachers – and the persistent barriers that prevent it from becoming standard, sustained practice. While many schools organize storytelling workshops, especially in the early years, few of these efforts survive the daily grind of timetables, assessments, and rigid curricula. The National Curriculum Framework 2023 (NCF-SE) offers us a fresh opportunity to shift this paradigm. By explicitly recognizing storytelling-based pedagogy as a core experiential strategy, NCF affirms that stories are not just nice – they are necessary.

To embed storytelling meaningfully in classrooms, we need more than policy endorsements lying in neatly spiral-bound documents in the reference section of the school library. We need mindset shifts, structural backing, and practical Indian models that show how stories support academic rigor. A recent study (Dwivedi & Sarangapani, 2023) found that while secondary teachers valued storytelling, many were unsure how to adapt it for the so-called ‘serious’ subjects or large classes. This points to a belief-practice gap, not an outright rejection of the approach. The truth is that stories are developmentally universal. What changes is not whether students enjoy them – it’s how we design their complexity and cognitive load. I’ve seen them work just as effectively in a history class on the Partition as in a physics class on forces. What matters is intentional design.

Yet, most teachers encounter storytelling only in workshops where facilitators perform engaging tales but rarely explain how to build story-led lessons. Teachers return inspired, but by Monday, the textbook takes over again. There’s many a slip between the workshop and the classroom!

Let’s examine this more clearly:

A useful intervention that we’ve made in our school is formalizing the Storytelling & Reading Lead (SRL) role. This isn’t a literary or activity coordinator – it’s a pedagogical specialist who works across grades and subjects to help teachers embed stories into the curriculum. As SRL, I don’t ‘teach stories’; I coach teachers how to use them.

In practice, that means:

  • Curriculum mapping – matching learning outcomes to story ideas.
  • Model lessons – co-planning and co-teaching with narrative hooks.
  • Resource curation – building a story bank aligned to topics and themes.
  • Teacher coaching – with observations, feedback, and reflection.
  • Community integration – inviting writers, storytellers, and subject experts.
  • Assessment design – helping reimagine evaluation through story-based formats.

While many teachers are enthusiastic, sustained and curriculum-aligned storytelling demands time, collaboration, and planning support – all of which are difficult to prioritize in a teacher’s overloaded schedule. That’s why an SRL is essential: not because teachers can’t do it, but because they shouldn’t have to do it alone.

Naturally, teachers ask: “How do I build a story-based lesson?” Many assume it requires a flair for theatre. But designing story-based lessons isn’t about performance – it’s about purposeful structure. One framework I’ve found effective is the five-step Panchpadi method, outlined in NCF and originally rooted in science pedagogy. Its five stages – Aditi, Bodh, Abhyas, Prayog, and Prasar – translate beautifully into story-based lesson design.

Here’s a simple toolkit you can use:

  1. Pin the concept: e.g., Newton’s laws, photosynthesis, Partition of India
  2. Find or craft a story: folktale, real event, analogy, case study, film, poetry
  3. Link it to a learning outcome: what competency or idea does it reveal?
  4. Use a five-part arc:
    • Aditi (Engage): Hook learners with a relatable question.
    • Bodh (Understand): Tell or read the story.
    • Abhyas (Explore): Engage with the story – drawing, questioning, role-playing.
    • Prayog (Apply): Apply the idea to a new context.
    • Prasar (Share): Retell or extend the story creatively.
  5. Assess creatively: through debates, diagrams, comic strips, character journals.

This model is particularly effective because it places the student’s meaning-making process at the centre, using the story not as entertainment but as cognitive structure.

But even the best designs need systemic support. School leadership must create time and space – in the timetable, in planning meetings, and in how teaching is evaluated. Professional learning should be ongoing and embedded in the school week – not left to one-off outsourced workshops. Budgets must include Indian children’s literature across languages, not just textbooks. Appraisal rubrics must recognize creative pedagogy.

Teachers must also be supported in forming in-school professional learning communities to share strategies, reflect on classroom practice, and build a collective repertoire. This is vital because external experts cannot fully address a school’s internal needs; meaningful development must be co-owned by teachers and in-house mentors. When teachers learn from one another, the impact is deeper and more sustainable.

We also need to rethink what we mean by a story. Many teachers limit themselves to fables or moral tales. But stories are everywhere: in courtroom transcripts, in weather reports, in cricket commentary, in political cartoons, and Instagram reels. If it has characters, a problem, and a sequence – it’s a story.

Here are a few examples we’ve used:

  • Science: Story cards tracing the discovery of force and motion from Galileo to Newton in physics for grade 9.
  • Math: A real snow leopard sighting in Spiti inspired a math story for grade 7, where two explorers' increase and decrease in altitude and temperature became the frame for introducing positive and negative integers.
  • History: Extracts from graphic nonfiction like People of the Indus and India Through Archaeology in grades 6 and 7 to introduce civilizations.

And with AI tools now at teachers’ fingertips, generating, adapting, or localizing stories has never been easier. AI can offer analogies, rewrite content for different age groups, and translate folktales into classroom-ready material. A teacher struggling to explain Newton’s third law of motion can now generate several metaphors within minutes – some rooted in local contexts, others drawn from global examples. Used well, AI reduces planning load and diversifies story sources. But it must be used with discernment. Over-dependence risks flattening voice, style, and cultural nuance. The goal isn’t to outsource creativity – it’s to amplify it, and free up teacher time for the responsive, relational work no AI can replace. In the words of Dr. Anuj Kapoor (Assistant Professor of Marketing at the Robert J. Trulaske, Sr. College of Business, Missouri), who studies the impact of AI on consumers and society, “You (the teacher) matter!”

NCF 2023 doesn’t just allow for storytelling – it makes a compelling case for it. It invites us to teach for competency, not just coverage; for depth, not just delivery. And it reminds us that stories are not a break from the curriculum – they are often the most direct route to it.

Stories must move from the margins of language periods to the heart of mainstream content. They must echo in science labs, anchor history debates, and reframe word problems in math. “Let me tell you a story…” should become a deliberate instructional strategy, not just a filler.

If you’re wondering where to begin, the potter, the donkey, and the three pots are still waiting.

References

  1. National Curriculum Framework for School Education (NCF-SE), 2023. Ministry of Education, Government of India. Retrieved from https://ncert.nic.in.
  2. Dwivedi, Ankit & Sarangapani, Padma. (2023). Perceptions and beliefs of Indian secondary school teachers regarding story telling. Pedagogies: An International Journal. 19. 1-14. 10.1080/1554480X.2023.2252811.
  3. NCERT (2021). Science Textbook for Class IX. National Council of Educational Research and Training.
  4. Economic Times (2024). Ghost of the mountains spotted in Spiti: Tourists capture rare snow leopard on camera in thrilling encounter. Retrieved from https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/magazines/panache/ghost-of-the-mountains-spotted-in-spiti-tourists-capture-rare-snow-leopard-on-camera-in-thrilling-encounter/articleshow/120970195.cms

Sample lesson plan

Newton’s Laws of Motion through the story of the stubborn potter

Grade: 9

Subject: Science

Chapter: Force and Laws of Motion (NCERT Chapter 8)

Duration: 2 periods (80 minutes)

Pedagogy: Storytelling, experiential learning

Learning objectives

  • Identify and explain Newton’s three laws of motion using a story.
  • Distinguish between balanced and unbalanced forces, and define inertia and momentum.
  • Relate force, mass, and acceleration through character actions and outcomes in the story.
  • Apply Newton’s laws to real-life and fictional situations.
  • Use diagrams, discussions, and models to demonstrate conceptual understanding.
  • Reflect on how stories can support science learning.

Materials required

  • Folktale (told orally or read aloud): The Stubborn Potter and His Donkey

Story summary: Once upon a time in a small town, there was a potter known for being very stubborn. One morning, he loaded three pots – two large ones on the left and a smaller one on the right – onto his donkey cart to deliver them to the market. The donkey refused to move. Angrily, the potter tugged it hard. The donkey lurched forward but wobbled under the uneven weight, though the pots somehow stayed in place. Midway through their journey, a sudden clap of thunder startled the donkey. He halted abruptly. The small pot on the right, unable to stop in time, continued moving and crashed to the ground. The potter sat on a rock and muttered, “Maybe I need a new donkey!” Or maybe it was time for him to learn a little physics.

  • Chart paper and markers
  • Props: a tray, coins, balls, strings, small weights
  • NCERT class 9 science textbook – chapter 8
  • Student worksheet (for story-to-law mapping)

Lesson flow (structured using the Panchpadi method)

1.    Aditi (Engage) – Begin by asking students: “Have you ever been on a bus that braked suddenly? What did your body do?” Let students share experiences that illustrate inertia.

2.    Bodh (Understand) – Narrate the adapted folktale: ‘The Potter, His Donkey, and Three Pots’. Pause at key moments to ask predictive or reflective questions.

3.    Abhyas (Explore) – In groups, students map each law of motion to an event in the story. Use storyboards or diagrams to reinforce understanding. Supplement with micro-demonstrations (e.g., flicking a card from under a coin).

  • Discuss: How is mass distributed? Where is force applied? When does acceleration occur? What is inertia? How does the donkey walk (action-reaction)?

4.    Prayog (Apply) – Ask students to redesign the potter’s cart or propose changes that reduce the risk of breakage, applying Newton’s laws. If the potter had distributed weight evenly or added wheels, how might the laws of motion have played out differently? Alternatively, write a new ending where the potter uses physics to solve the problem.

5.    Prasar (Share) – Students present their story-law mappings, alternate endings, or comic strips. Students reflect on: ‘Which law was hardest to identify and why?’

Mapping story elements to Newton’s Laws

After mapping story events to each Newton law, refer to textbook examples directly:

  • Compare donkey’s inertia to marble on inclined plane (Fig. 8.5c)
  • Link pot crash to striker/carom coin analogy (Activity 8.1)
  • Use mass of pot vs donkey to discuss inertia (Activity 8.2)

Assessment strategies

  • Group presentations explaining the connection between the story and Newton’s laws.
  • Exit slips asking students to write one real-world example of each law.
  • Creative submissions like comic strips, alternate endings, or skits demonstrating the laws.
  • Peer and teacher feedback on presentations and participation.

Extension ideas

  • Ask students to find or create another folktale that could be used to explain a science concept.
  • Link with language class to develop a dramatic script of the story with embedded scientific commentary.

Additional notes

Your folk tale lesson covers:

Sample worksheet

Worksheet: Newton’s Laws through the story of the stubborn potter

Name: ___________________________ Class: 9_____ Date: ____________

  1. Quick recall

From what you remember of the story, answer the following questions:

a) Why did the donkey refuse to move?

b) What happened when the potter tugged the donkey harder?

c) What made the last pot fall and break?

2. Match Newton’s Laws to the story

Fill in the blanks to connect story events with the three laws of motion.

3. Apply what you learned

Choose any one of the following and complete it on a separate sheet:

  1. a) Design a better pot-carrying solution for the potter based on Newton’s laws.
  2. b) Write an alternate ending to the story using scientific reasoning.
  3. c) Make a comic strip called “Newton Goes to the Market” showing the same story.

4. Exit reflection

In your own words, explain how this story helped you understand Newton’s laws better.

Deeptha Vivekanand is the Storytelling and Reading Lead at a private school in Chandigarh. Her work centres on designing story-based learning experiences and supporting teacher development through reflective practice. She can be reached at deeptha.vivekanand@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Deeptha Vivekanand
Can storytelling move from workshops into everyday classrooms? Deeptha Vivekanand writes that stories are essential for deep learning.

item.title: Who is left standing?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Suma Vivekanandan

Addition and subtraction of integers can become a bit daunting for children particularly when they encounter negative numbers. So, I devised a few fun games to help children easily grasp the concept of adding and subtracting integers. I hope these games prove useful in your classrooms too.

I used to teach in a co-education school and hence I followed the method below, but you can make your own rules. If the class size is small, all students can participate, if it is large, you can divide them into groups and have each group play a game.

In my classroom, we wrote out two chits and had one boy and one girl pick them. According to the chits they picked, the boys got to represent positive numbers and the girls, negative numbers. I asked the boys (positive numbers) to stand always looking to the east and the girls (negative numbers) looking to the west.

Game 1

-4 + 3

Ask 4 girls to stand in a row (looking to the west) and 3 boys to stand looking to the east. So the line of girls and the line of boys will be facing each other.

One boy and one girl each make a pair and get nullified.

Now who is left?

One girl. Girls are negative.

So the answer is -1

Game 2

6+ (-2)

6 boys in a row

2 girls in a row.

2 pairs get nullified.

Who is left? 4 boys.

Answer: +4

Game 3

7-(-4)

7 boys stand in a row looking towards the east

4 girls stand looking towards the west.

Now the teacher has to give clear instructions. Opposite of (-) is positive. The opposite direction of west is east. So, the girls also have to stand looking towards the east.

Now all the children are standing towards the east that is (+)

So the answer is +11

Game 4

-5- (-4)

5 girls stand looking towards the west

Another 4 girls come and stand looking towards the west (-). Since two negatives make a positive, 4 girls have to turn to the east.

Remember the rule, whoever stands face to face is nullified.

Now only one girl is standing towards the east.

Answer is: -1

Game 5

-5+ (-3)

5 girls stand looking towards the west

Another 3 girls come and stand looking towards the west.

Now add them together.

The total is 8 girls standing looking towards the west.

The answer: -8

As they keep playing these games, students will pick up the rules of adding and subtracting positive and negative numbers.

Suma Vivekanandan is a former Primary School Coordinator and math teacher at Atul Vidyalaya, Atul. She can be reached at vivekanandansuma@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Suma Vivekanandan
Adding and subtracting integers can also be fun. Suma Vivekanandan shares easy and engaging games to help students understand the concepts and make learning enjoyable.

item.title: Mentor teachers: catalysts in empathy
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nivedita Bedadur

This article is the first in a series of articles on mentoring as a strategy for developing academic leaders. The series will explore models of teacher mentoring in India in state board schools, Kendriya Vidyalayas, private schools, and of school heads. The series showcases best practices in the field, some of them predating NCF 2020 and The Blue Book on Mentoring.*

A mentor is defined as a knowledgeable and experienced guide, a trusted ally and advocate, and a caring role model. (Blue Book on NMM page 2) A widely accepted modern view is that mentoring is a developmental relationship with relational and instrumental aspects. It is a two-way street, where both mentor and mentee learn and grow. Mentoring can be defined simply as sharing knowledge, skills, and experience in order to encourage and empower another person. In contexts of teacher development, this process involves enhancing teachers’ autonomy to develop for themselves, increasing their ability and willingness to take control of their own learning rather than judging or directly advising them, or giving them all the answers. (Smith, R 2020).

What is the National Mission on Mentoring for India?

The National Mission on Mentoring is a mission to develop a pool of experienced mentors at all levels of learning to share their expertise with teachers, from new inductees to experienced teachers. It envisages mentoring for personal and professional growth of both the mentor and the mentee, a process which capacitates the mentee in ‘on the ground’ problem solving, pedagogical innovation, and practical skill, creating empowered and confident school leaders.

What are the attributes of a mentor?

A mentor must be an experienced professional. She must have both theoretical and practical knowledge as well as empathy. The most important skills that a mentor needs to develop are active listening, creating safe sharing spaces, and critical questioning. A mentor needs to empower by refraining from offering solutions, but help mentees arrive at them by engaging in reflective practice.

Why is mentoring so important?

Mentoring has been looked at as a possible solution to the policy-practice puzzle that Indian education is plagued with. We are all aware that government policies do not translate into implementation on the ground due to several reasons. The Indian education system is one of the largest in the world with nearly 14.71 lakh schools, more than 1.01 crore teachers and 24.69 crore students from diverse socio-economic backgrounds and contexts. (UDISE plus.gov.in 2024-25). The alignment between the government and different educational institutions at the state level, the contexts and cultures of different territories in India, the translation of policies into doables are some of the roadblocks in implementation. In this article, we will examine the Delhi Government Mentor Teacher (MT) model, which tried to overcome the difficulties of implementing a government policy to improve learning levels in public schools in Delhi.

Chunauti and Buniyad^ – Delihi’s Mentor Teacher Model of Education Reform: where policy translated into practice

There are 1534 MCD (Municipal Corporation of Delhi) schools in Delhi, 850,000 students, and 19000 teachers. Almost 80 percent of the students belong to low socio-economic status and around 50 percent are first generation learners (Kaushik, 2024). In 2016, the Delhi government took up the challenge of improving the learning levels of 1.6 million students in MCD schools by mentoring more than 1000 secondary teachers with the help of about 200 Mentor Teachers (MTs) and Teacher Development Coordinators (TDCs). A massive recruitment drive was conducted to attract teacher leaders to apply for the mentor teacher post. Mentors were selected after a stringent training process and were trained in leadership skills, innovative pedagogies, and mentoring. A deep dive immersion in best practices was organized through visits to different education institutes in India. To share the load of mentoring teachers and achieve the goal of developing teacher resource groups at the school level, TDCs were appointed in 2017.

What were the roles and responsibilities of mentor teachers and teacher development coordinators?

Each MT was in charge of four to five schools. They reported at the district level and did not take up actual classroom work. They visited one school every day, held teacher meetings, met with the head teacher, gave on-site support to teachers through classroom observations, assisted in lesson planning, and held teacher development workshops. They generated reports based on their visits and liasoned with the education department. The role of the MT extended beyond the classroom, they acted as friends, guides, and facilitators for teachers. They were involved in a massive movement of curriculum reform and content development, lesson planning, and innovative practices.

The TDCs were stationed in schools. They observed classes daily, identified the learning needs of students, provided feedback to address academic challenges and developed an academic resource group at the school level.

Both these posts were at the same level and individuals were trained to develop a symbiotic relationship with teachers.

The MTs and the TDCs were primarily responsible for liaisoning between the government and schools to translate policies on ground. Their role was to discuss challenges on the ground with the higher authorities. To facilitate this, a weekly cadence was set up at the zonal level, monthly cadence at the district level, and bi monthly cadence at the state level. This alignment was ensured for two-way communication.

The model of mentorship adopted by the Delhi government very specifically discouraged monitoring. The MTs and TDCs were expected to face and work through challenges. Top-down instructions and lecturing was not encouraged. The academic resource group learnt through sharing and reflection and discussion and research.

The challenges of catalysing change

Resistance to labelling students

The MTs and TDCs faced many challenges. The initial challenge of building relationships with heads of schools and teachers was overcome with the appointment of TDCs at the school level. But as innovations evolved and other players, experts from the field got involved, the challenges grew. The government adopted the TaRL philosophy of teaching at the right level and grouped children according to their learning levels into three groups Neo Nishtha: dropouts and failing class 9 more than once, Nishtha: students scoring less than 33%, and Pratibha: students scoring more than 33%. The teachers conveyed their strong objection to this because they felt it was like labelling students. Master trainers and TDCs helped teachers understand the intention behind the grouping, which was flexible and not fixed. Parents and children too were unhappy about the grouping. The MTs and TDCs were instrumental in creating an understanding amongst the stakeholders.

Empowering teachers: I’m the captain of my ship

The first set of materials for Teaching at the Right level were prepared at the district level. Teachers were unhappy with the materials. They expressed their concerns vocally. The MTs carried these voices to the highest level asserting that no one knew their students, their context, and their needs better than the teachers. A massive subject-wise summer workshop was held at the district level, in which the teachers with the help of MTs and TDCs brought out the celebrated Pragati series of resource material.

Personality matters: from falling to flying

The MTs faced a variety of challenges. They became the guardian angels of their respective schools. When the sorting of students happened, the attendance of Neo Nishthas dropped alarmingly. Every MT and TDC tried different methods of enhancing student attendance. Two MTs shared innovative practices of increasing student attendance. One MT organized free health camps for families and grabbed the opportunity of convincing parents to send their children to school. Another used the loud speaker of a nearby mosque to remind parents to send their children to school.

Conclusion

A teacher says, “Last five or six years, there has been a paradigm shift in our schools. Now the teachers have developed the practice of discussing academic issues, issues related to the classroom” (Thakur & Gupta, 2025). The Delhi Government Mentor Teacher Model did not guarantee success. There were many ups and downs along the way. Yet, this model changed the grammar of teacher development by providing support and then insisting on improved accountability. It empowered a simple classroom teacher to lead a group by widening her horizon, enhancing her knowledge base, and encouraging innovation.

References

  1. Aiyar, Y., Davis, V., Govindan, G., & Kapoor, T. (2021). *Rewriting the grammar of education systems: Delhi’s education reform (A tale of creative resistance and creative disruption).* Research on Improving Systems of Education (RISE).
  2. Batra, P. (2009). Teacher education in India: Theoretical and policy perspectives. *Contemporary Education Dialogue, 6*(2), 121–156.
  3. Boston Consulting Group. (n.d.). *School education reforms in Delhi 2015–2020: Interventions handbook.* https://web-assets.bcg.com
  4. Childress, D. (2021). *Supporting teachers from the middle tier: The Teacher Development Coordinator Program in Delhi.* International Institute for Educational Planning & UNESCO.
  5. Gavari, A. (2019). *Delhi revolution (The Pragati Series).* Azim Premji Foundation. https://publications.azimpremjifoundation.org
  6. Joshi, M. (2019, October 21). Mentor teacher – Delhi government’s sauce for education reforms. *The Indian Express.*
  7. Kaushik, M., & Chaudhary, S. (2024). *A case study from Delhi Project Management Unit.* Vibha vibha.org
  8. State Council of Education Research and Training. (n.d.). *SCERT Delhi.* https://scert.delhi.gov.in
  9. Shanthakumar, V., Khandpur, N. K. D., & Jain, S. (2018). *Political change and education reforms: Lessons from Delhi under Aam Aadmi Party.* Azim Premji University.
  10. Thakur, T., & Gupta, Y. (2025). An evaluation of the mentor teacher program in Delhi government schools. *National Journal of Education, XXIII*(10).
  11. Yadav, S., & Kanvaria, V. K. (2019). *Towards transformative science pedagogy: Critical insights from government schools of NCT.* Vibha. https://vibha.org

*The Blue Book on Mentoring is a book of guidelines by the Government of India brought out by the National Council for Teacher Education on March 9 2024 to draw out the road map for the National Mission on Mentoring.

^The Chunauti Program 2016 to 2017, Buniyad 2017 to 2023.

Nivedita Bedadur is happiest when she is with children and teachers, learning, unlearning, and teaching. Over years of mentoring and leading teacher capacity development, she has refined her listening and relearning muscle. Presently, she is researching for her upcoming book on the lives of the marginalized people of Konkan. She can be reached at nitavbedadur@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nivedita Bedadur
What happens when mentoring replaces monitoring? Nivedita Bedadur writes about how empathy-driven teacher mentorship translated education policy into classroom practice in Delhi.

item.title: Unfurling pride: rediscovering India’s national identity
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Radhika Gupta

Every school morning in India begins with a familiar choreography of patriotism – children straightening their posture, their voices weaving into the national anthem in imperfect unison, and their eyes lifted towards the national flag. Though this is a well-rehearsed ritual, when I ask my students where our flag came from, or why we have lions on our emblem and not horses or eagles, they have no answer. We teach children to respect the national symbols, but we rarely tell them why.

Kavita Mandana’s four illustrated books – The Story of India’s National Flag, The Story of India’s National Emblem, The Story of India’s National Anthem, and The Story of India’s National Symbols feel like an antidote to the thin history our textbooks offer. Most textbook chapters on national identity list out facts like ingredients on a package: tiger (national animal), lotus (national flower), tricolour (flag), and so on. No context is given, no wonder is ignited, no heartbreak is felt while walking through the pain of our patriotic ancestors, and hence, it does not evoke any pride.

Mandana’s books restore the missing heartbeat. These slim, beautifully illustrated volumes do what history teaching should always aim to do: bring the past alive. We say “Ashoka Chakra” without giving a thought to Ashoka’s life transformation. We see the lion capital on our currency without imagining the Mauryan Emperor searching for ways to spread peace after a devastating war. It delights the educator in me to see how these books combine meticulous research with child-friendly curiosity. The Emblem’s journey reads like an archaeological thriller. One can picture gigantic stone pillars being quarried near the Ganges, floated downstream on barges, and then transported unimaginable distances without cranes or trucks. Mandana explores why Ashoka chose lions, and why they face different directions. The emblem is not just artwork; it is a deeply symbolic reminder of what governance must stand for: power balanced with compassion. A question students frequently ask is why we use animals as symbols. This book answers such questions with a rich context, telling us about the lion roaring justice and the bull representing steadfastness.

One of the chapters in The Story of India’s National Flag talks about how the earliest versions of the Indian flag were stitched by freedom fighters in secrecy. Imagine students discovering that the symbol they proudly hoist today was once banned. And that it travelled from Bengal to Andhra, changed colours, acquired the charkha, and finally, the chakra. Mandana takes children back to a time when even the gesture of raising a flag was a rebellious act. In one village in Karnataka, young people raised a homemade flag in defiance of the British only to face police bullets for what we now treat as a routine civic act. The flag gave us an identity in a milieu when our self-esteem as a nation was brutally mutilated. It went through many transformations and was stitched with the fine threads of courage.

We sing Jana Gana Mana everyday but seldom talk about how a reluctant Tagore didn’t want his composition to become a song of nationalism. Instead, he wanted it to be a call to humanity. The National Anthem, thus, wasn’t born in a quiet room, but amidst political uproar, creative debates, and ingenious acts of resistance. Mandana recounts how early recording technology was smuggled into living rooms by visionaries who captured speeches and songs so that they could rally thousands, even when the speakers themselves were locked behind bars.

Finally, the crown jewel of unexpected learning: India has a National Microbe! And no, it wasn’t chosen by politicians or experts. In fact, it was voted by children across the country. Imagine telling your class that bacterium – yes, those tiny invisible life forms – can represent India on the world stage! It shifts patriotism from monuments to microscopes, expanding what it means ‘to belong’.

Progressive education teaches children to ask better questions instead of simply memorizing answers. After reading the flag book, one of my students asked me: “If the colours and symbols on the flag changed so many times, how do we know this version will stay forever?” This question holds more learning than any list of national symbols ever could. It acknowledges that identity evolves. It also shows that democracy is a constant work in progress. It enumerates that what we honour today has emerged from debates, disagreements, and imagination, and can be refined by future citizens.

The visuals in this series deserve a special mention. Archival photographs sit alongside modern illustrations, allowing children to move back and forth in time effortlessly. My students spent substantial time observing the early versions of our flag, some with spinning wheels while others with crescents and stars, generating hypotheses about why they may have been changed. To my mind, this is literacy…this is critical thinking…and this is history refusing to sit still.

As educators, we often underestimate how deeply children yearn to understand the why of the world. I have seen classrooms transform when we stop teaching patriotism as posture and start teaching it as perspective. Mandana ignites a fire by making history relatable, visual, and surprisingly emotional. Her writing encourages children to see themselves as part of a living story.

Some of my favourite classroom moments inspired by the series: Students designed “future flags” of India, each colour representing a value they want the nation to embrace. They created mini-exhibitions explaining symbols of their own making and fiercely defended why their choices mattered. Students compared national anthems of different countries and reflected on what each nation chooses to celebrate in song.

In a time when nationalism can quickly become noise that may be loud, but absolutely shallow, we need quieter, deeper engagements. Books like these remind children that history is not guaranteed. People fought for every inch of dignity we have now inherited. If we want children to safeguard democracy, they must know its origin story.

Kavita Mandana’s books are a must for every school. They belong in classrooms because they help children understand the country they stand up for every morning. They acquaint students with the struggle stitched into our national flag, the courage hidden in our anthem, the craftsmanship that shapes our emblem, and the rich diversity behind every national symbol we so often take for granted. When pride comes from such understanding, it becomes thoughtful and lasting. These books give children as well as teachers a fresh perspective to see history as a living inheritance. And that, I feel, is the kind of patriotic learning our classrooms deserve!

Radhika Gupta is Principal at Manav Rachna International School, Mohali. She can be reached at rads.gupta@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Radhika Gupta
Children sing the national anthem every day, but it’s equally important for them to know and understand what the words mean, writes Radhika Gupta.
