item.title: Nurturing mathematical understanding through formative assessment
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

From the 1960s and 1970s, the terms formative assessment and summative assessment entered the language of educators – formative assessment being assessment that takes place during teaching to make adjustments to the teaching process and summative assessment being assessment at the end of a unit or term to convey student progress (Western and Northern Canadian Protocol for Collaboration in Education, 2006).

In our daily interactions with students, we are constantly seeking ways to understand their grasp of mathematical concepts. Formative assessment emerges as a powerful tool in this endeavour, acting as a continuous compass that guides both our teaching and our students' learning journeys (Carnegie Mellon University, n.d.). It's about more than just correcting answers; it's about actively supervising learning with efficient and constructive feedback, allowing us to adapt our curriculum in real-time to meet the evolving needs of our students (Carnegie Mellon University, n.d.). This dynamic approach transforms teaching into a simultaneously creative and responsive process.

Projects as formative assessments

One particularly enriching formative assessment practice involves projects that culminate in personal presentations. By providing a task or project with differentiated outcomes and allowing students to choose their mode of presentation – be it through PowerPoint, a written document, a model, or any other medium – we unlock a wellspring of creativity (Western and Northern Canadian Protocol for Collaboration in Education, 2006). Each time we assign such a task, we are often met with delightful surprises as students infuse their unique perspectives and talents into their work.

For instance, when introducing the fascinating concept of 'pi' and its role in the area of a circle, instead of simply stating the value, we can initiate a journey of discovery. We can pose trigger questions that encourage students to independently seek information about pi. This initial exploration can then lead to engaging group discussions where students clarify their understanding and share their findings with one another. Finally, each student can synthesize their learning into an individual presentation for the class, receiving valuable feedback from both the teacher and their peers. This approach, adaptable to various group sizes, allows for individual expression and a deeper, more personal connection with the mathematical concept. Witnessing students confidently present their unique understanding is truly a rewarding experience.

Other formative assessments

Beyond such projects, numerous other formative assessment strategies, each offering a distinct window into student learning, can be seamlessly integrated into middle school math classrooms:

  • Thumbs up/down/sideways: A quick check for understanding after explaining a rule or concept.
    • Example (Properties of Triangles): After discussing the angle sum property of a triangle, ask: "Do the angles of any triangle add up to 180 degrees?" Students can respond with thumbs up (yes), thumbs down (no), or sideways (unsure), allowing for immediate clarification of any misconceptions.
  • Traffic light cards: Students use coloured cards to signal their level of confidence with a topic.
    • Example (Linear Equations): After practicing one-step linear equations, ask students to hold up a green card if they feel confident, yellow if they are somewhat unsure, and red if they need more help.
  • Think-write-pair-share: Encouraging individual reflection followed by collaborative discussion.
    • Example (Integers): Pose the question: "Explain in your own words what negative numbers represent." Students first write their thoughts down, then discuss with a partner to refine their understanding, and finally share their explanations with the class.
  • One-minute paper: A brief written reflection at the end of a lesson segment.
    • Example (Fractions and Decimals): At the end of a lesson on converting fractions to decimals, ask students to write down the key connection between the two concepts or a question they still have.
  • Concept mapping: Visually representing the relationships between mathematical ideas. Online tools can enhance this activity.
    • Example (Types of Quadrilaterals): Students can create a concept map illustrating the hierarchy and properties of squares, rectangles, parallelograms, rhombuses, and trapezoids.
  • Error analysis: Identifying and explaining mistakes in worked-out problems.
    • Example (Simplifying Algebraic Expressions): Provide students with an incorrectly simplified expression and ask them to pinpoint the error and provide the correct simplification.
  • Hinge questions: Strategic multiple-choice questions to assess understanding of a key concept before moving forward.
    • Example (Percentages): After teaching how to find the percentage of a quantity, ask: "To find 25% of 80, you should: (a) multiply 80 by 25, (b) divide 80 by 25, (c) multiply 80 by 0.25, (d) divide 80 by 0.25." The incorrect options often reflect common misunderstandings.
  • Show your work (with Focus): Emphasizing the process and specific strategies used in problem-solving.
    • Example (Area of Composite Shapes): When asking students to find the area of an L-shaped figure, instruct them to "Show two different ways you can decompose the shape into simpler rectangles to find the total area."
  • Peer assessment with empathy: Guide students to provide constructive feedback to their classmates in a supportive manner. After a presentation, encourage peers to share "one good thing" and "one area for improvement" using kind and helpful language. Anonymous voting for a favourite presentation can also foster a sense of appreciation for different approaches.
  • Self-reflection: Providing dedicated time for students to assess their own learning and compare their current understanding with their progress over time (Western and Northern Canadian Protocol for Collaboration in Education, 2006). This metacognitive practice empowers them to take ownership of their learning journey.

 

Why formative assessment?

While the integration of formative and summative assessments holds immense value (Looney, 2011), their practical implementation can be challenging due to factors like the need for technological support for efficient data collection and the prevailing societal emphasis on final grades (Popham, 2005). However, within our own classrooms, we possess the agency to champion formative assessments. The continuous feedback they provide not only clarify concepts but also cultivates a sense of confidence and anticipation in our students. Instead of approaching the math class with apprehension, they begin to see it as a space for exploration, discovery, and growth.

Conclusion

By embracing formative assessment as a guiding light in our teaching practice, we move beyond simply imparting knowledge to nurturing a genuine understanding and appreciation for mathematics. It is in these daily interactions, these moments of checking for understanding and providing timely feedback, that we truly empower our students to become confident and capable mathematical thinkers.

References

  • Carnegie Mellon University. (n.d.). Formative vs summative assessment. https://www.cmu.edu/teaching/assessment/basics/formative-summative.html
  • Looney, J. (2011). Integrating formative and summative assessment. OECD Education Working Papers, (58). https://doi.org/10.1787/5kghx3kbl734-en
  • Popham, W. J. (2005). F for assessment. Edutopia. http://www.edutopia.org/f-for-assessment
  • Western and Northern Canadian Protocol for Collaboration in Education. (2006). Rethinking classroom assessment with purpose in mind. https://www.edu.gov.mb.ca/k12/assess/wncp/full_doc.pdf

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
Beyond tests and grades, Monica Kochar shares formative assessment strategies that can turn math classrooms into spaces for feedback, reflection, and growth.

item.title: Curtains up on science: integrating performance arts with biology
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monideepa Mukhopadhyay

In today’s rapidly changing educational landscape, science teachers face the constant challenge of keeping students engaged, especially in subjects like biology, where abstract concepts and intricate processes can sometimes appear distant and difficult to grasp. At the same time, students are growing up in an era rich in visual, performative, and collaborative media. How can science instruction evolve to meet the needs of modern learners? One powerful, innovative answer lies in the integration of theatre and performance arts into science classrooms.

In India, this pedagogical innovation is not only gaining popularity but is also backed by strong policy support from the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, which aims to encourage holistic, experiential, and interdisciplinary learning. As a biology facilitator, I have employed role plays, theatre-based teaching, and even interpretive dance to teach grade 8 and 9 students complex topics such as types of reproduction in animals, cell organelles, and the endocrine system.

What was the outcome? Increased student engagement, deeper conceptual understanding, and an atmosphere of enthusiasm that traditional lectures often fail to inspire.

NEP 2020 and the emphasis on arts integration

India’s National Education Policy 2020 has laid significant emphasis on making education holistic, enjoyable, and effective. It proposes a transformation in both curricular structures and pedagogical strategies, with a strong recommendation for the integration of arts into science and other disciplines. By embedding theatre into the science curriculum, teachers not only fulfill NEP’s vision but also foster a deeper emotional and intellectual engagement with learning.

Why theatre and science?

The idea of combining theatre with science might seem unconventional, but it is rooted in sound educational psychology. Performance arts, especially when applied in classroom contexts, can simultaneously activate multiple modes of learning:

  1. Concrete visualization of abstract concepts: Scientific topics such as the endocrine system or cellular structures are invisible to the naked eye. Enacting these as characters in a skit or play makes them visible and memorable.
  2. Enhanced kinesthetic learning: Many students are kinesthetic learners who benefit from movement-based activities. Drama and dance anchor abstract concepts in bodily experience, helping these students absorb information more effectively.
  3. Teamwork and communication skills: Collaborative performances promote group learning and encourage students to express themselves clearly, thereby enhancing their communication skills – a core competency for 21st century learners.
  4. Bridging cultural contexts: Theatre has always been a part of Indian heritage. Using regional forms such as Yakshagana, Nautanki, or Therukoothu to convey scientific principles helps students relate better to the material while honouring their cultural roots.
  5. Increased retention and recall: Students often retain information better when they have “experienced” it through enactment. A lesson performed is a lesson remembered.
  6. Fostering empathy and critical thinking: Science isn't just facts; it's about processes, consequences, and sometimes ethical decisions. Theatre allows students to explore the implications of scientific knowledge through dramatization, thereby deepening their understanding.

Case studies from Delhi Public School, Nadergul

Over the past academic year, I implemented three theatre-based modules in grade 8 and 9 biology classrooms. Each was designed to convey specific scientific concepts through student-led performances.

Case study 1: Cell city – understanding the cell as a functional system

In a unit on cell structure and function, students participated in a play titled "Cell City." Each student assumed the role of a cell organelle – nucleus, mitochondria, ribosome, endoplasmic reticulum, etc. The class transformed into a stage representing a bustling city, where the organelles had distinct jobs and interdependencies.

  • The nucleus acted as the mayor.
  • The mitochondria were powerhouses supplying energy.
  • The ribosomes were depicted as factories producing goods.
  • The Golgi apparatus served as the city’s postal service.

The dramatization helped students understand not just the function of each part but also how these organelles interact in a living system.

Result: Students performed significantly better in assessments following the activity, with nearly 90% correctly identifying and explaining organelle functions compared to 55% before the intervention.

Case study 2: Reproduction dance-drama – making the invisible visible

When addressing the sensitive yet important topic of types of reproduction in animals, I employed an interpretive dance-drama format. Students choreographed movements representing binary fission, budding, sexual reproduction, and fertilization using fabric props and simple choreography.

  • A student splitting in two represented binary fission,
  • Group formations and detachment symbolized budding,
  • Partner dances and narration illustrated internal fertilization.

This approach allowed students to engage with the topic in a respectful, inclusive, and creative manner.

Result: Not only did students report feeling more comfortable discussing reproduction, but their understanding of the differences between asexual and sexual reproduction improved markedly.

Case study 3: The hormone courtroom – endocrine system role play

In this activity, the classroom was turned into a courtroom. Hormones were put on trial, and students took on the roles of judges, attorneys, glands, and hormones. For example:

  • Insulin was accused of mismanaging blood sugar,
  • Thyroxine was interrogated about metabolic changes,
  • Adrenaline was examined for causing stress responses.

Cross-examinations, witness testimonies, and final verdicts helped students understand hormonal regulation, the interaction between glands, and feedback loops.

Result: Post-performance reflections showed that the students could articulate the endocrine system functions with depth and even relate hormonal imbalances to common disorders like diabetes or hyperthyroidism.

Classroom applications – from concept to curtain

Here are some topic-specific ideas for integrating theatre into biology instruction

Tips for teachers – getting started with performance arts in science

  1. Start with simple scripts: You don’t need elaborate stage settings. Begin with a simple skit, perhaps a five-minute enactment at the end of a chapter.
  2. Involve students in planning: Let students write the script or choreograph the performance. It increases ownership and learning.
  3. Use existing cultural forms: Use local dance, theatre, and storytelling methods. This not only enhances engagement but also fulfills NEP’s cultural mandate.
  4. Work collaboratively: Partner with your school’s art teachers to brainstorm ideas and co-teach lessons.
  5. Assess creatively: Use rubrics to assess understanding, collaboration, creativity, and communication.
  6. Encourage reflection: After each performance, have students write reflection journals or create concept maps to solidify their learning.
  7. Document the process: Record videos or take photos of the performances to review learning and showcase student creativity.

Overcoming challenges

The scientific stage awaits

Theatre in science teaching is not just an experiment – it is a revolution in pedagogical thinking. By transforming classrooms into creative spaces where performance and science intersect, educators can unlock new levels of curiosity, empathy, and understanding among students. At Delhi Public School, Nadergul, it clearly showed how performance-based learning can turn even the most complex biological topics into accessible and enjoyable learning experiences. The excitement, the collaborative spirit, and the glow of understanding on students’ faces are testimony to the power of creative teaching.

Moreover, this approach aligns seamlessly with the National Education Policy 2020, supporting its vision of an interdisciplinary, culturally rooted, and student-centric education system. It empowers students not only with scientific knowledge but also with essential life skills such as teamwork, public speaking, critical thinking, and emotional intelligence.

The shift from passive note-taking to active dramatization fosters an environment where learners don’t just memorize biology – they live it. Students become cell organelles, hormones, and chromosomes; they dance the cycle of life and debate in hormone courtrooms. These performances become anchors in their memory, helping them recall and apply what they’ve learned far beyond the classroom.

To fellow educators: integrating theatre and performance arts may seem daunting at first, but it starts with a single skit, a single role-play, or a simple narration. Over time, it can evolve into a transformative teaching methodology that reshapes not just how we teach science, but how our students experience it. As educators, let us embrace the stage, script, and spotlight to bring science to life – one act at a time.

Dr. Monideepa Mukhopadhyay is a faculty member of biology in the Science Department at Delhi Public School Nadergul, Hyderabad. She can be reached at bhattacharjeemonideepa9@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monideepa Mukhopadhyay
Incorporating drama, dance, and role-play into biology education can make abstract scientific concepts into memorable experiences that students never forget. According to Dr. Monideepa Mukhopadhyay, performance-based learning is reshaping biology classrooms under NEP 2020.

item.title: Integrating science and social science
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Om Kaustubh Bagwe

In India, most educational boards have structured their curricula so that each subject has its own textbook, and each subject is taught by a specific teacher during a designated "period." This also applies to subjects like science and social studies. However, if we look at these subjects more broadly, we realize that although the concepts taught in them may seem distinct, many a times, we often apply them interchangeably and simultaneously in our daily lives without even realizing it.

For example, you want to drink a special variety of tea. What is the process involved? First, you go to a shop where special varieties of tea are sold. You ask for the tea you want. The shopkeeper might ask you questions about its origin, i.e., place of cultivation and the quantity you want. You might negotiate the price or check in other shops for a better deal. Once you have the tea you want, you will make a "solution" of tea leaves and water and boil this solution so that the flavour of leaves is absorbed by the water, a process known as "solid-liquid extraction." Finally, you will “filter” this hot solution to separate the liquid from the tea leaves. Just to sip your favourite drink, look at the different tasks you had to perform; they involved concepts learnt across multiple subjects such as geography, economics, mathematics, and chemistry.

To encourage students to recognize how knowledge from different disciplines such as science and social science can be integrated and applied to real-world scenarios, integrated learning can be an effective option that schools can include in their curriculum. Research has shown that students who experience an integrated curriculum are more motivated to learn, find their studies more meaningful, and do as well if not better on standardized tests (Nollmeyer et al., 2016). Integrated curriculum means “bridge building between different school subjects or building an interdisciplinary curriculum with the objective of making learning more holistic by increasing curricular coherence” (Niemelä, 2022, p.228).

Science and social studies may differ with respect to the content and type of knowledge they offer, but several concepts taught in both disciplines are interrelated. For example, in geography, a chapter may discuss animals found in a specific region, but to find out how a particular animal survives (physiology, anatomy, metabolism, adaptations, genetics, etc.) in that region (habitat), students may have to refer to their science textbooks. In this case, both subjects offer information about a common topic (that is, the animal), but the information provided by each subject differs.

What does integration achieve?

Building a holistic perspective

Social sciences have the potential to bring a human-centred perspective to STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics) challenges, which can help address issues like social equity, behaviour, and cultural differences. This can help students become effective problem solvers and address global challenges better.

Consider the topic “Energy from fire”. The teacher can begin the class by asking students basic questions related to the topic. As students respond and become more comfortable with the concept, the teacher can introduce a discussion on when humans discovered fire, when and why they began harnessing it, and where evidence of its use has been found. After this, the teacher can explain the nature of fire and how humans discovered different techniques to generate fire throughout history. As the teacher discusses different techniques to generate fire, the students will understand how humans made technological advancements and also how these advancements led to the growth of scientific knowledge. Such discussions can offer students valuable insight into how humans have applied problem-solving approaches throughout history by making use of available resources, and how scientific advancements were achieved. One can also gain an insights into the impact of these scientific advancements on society leading to the evolution of different cultures around the world.

Enhanced creativity and innovation

Collaboration between STEM and social sciences can foster interdisciplinary thinking, promote creativity and innovation by merging technical expertise with insights into human behaviour, needs, and societal trends.

Generation of waste is a common phenomenon in any society, with its levels ranging from waste generated by an individual to that produced by an entire town. Ineffective waste management is a significant issue faced by many communities. Students can be encouraged to use waste materials from home/school to build artefacts discussed in their curriculum such as rain gauges, seismographs, etc. However, one must also note that such activities are also criticized for their inability to convey core concepts around waste. Therefore, students can be encouraged to look for problems around them, such as the lack of space for gardening in school, the absence of designated bins for recyclable electronic waste, etc., and be asked to come up with solutions using waste items. Students may also undertake field visits to understand how waste collectors and recyclers segregate and recycle different materials. Such activities will not only help students understand different types of waste generated but also their impact on the environment.

Informed citizenry

An integrated science and social studies curriculum can help students understand how scientific knowledge can be applied to deal with problems in society.

Social studies also includes civics, which mainly deals with the rights and duties of citizens and how to participate in civic life. Civics can be integrated with STEM, where students can be encouraged to research common problems faced by people in their community such as the prevalence of diseases in an area, repairing broken bridges, economic distress, lack of public spaces for rejuvenation, etc. Students can then be asked to suggest solutions to the problems and convey them creatively through 3D models, debates, etc. (Fultz, 2021).

This approach will foster a sense of awareness and equip future citizens with the knowledge to make informed decisions and actively participate in addressing global challenges.

Examples of integrated learning

  • Discussion of socioscientific issues (SSI) in science class is known to have some impact on students’ ability to understand others’ perspectives as well as developing active assertions (Chung et al., 2016). SSI topics may include critical issues like water pollution, transmission of diseases, human-wildlife conflicts, etc., where the science may be discussed with students along with the social aspects.
  • Design problems have the potential to bring together many subjects. If students are encouraged to ideate solutions for real-world problems, they will need to dwell into humanities, maths, science, etc. For example, when students are assigned a problem such as repairing a broken bridge in a village or a city, students engage in tasks such as researching the bridge’s location and its purpose, selecting the materials to be used for construction, understanding local geographical and weather conditions, and taking note of economical and cultural considerations while designing the bridge. Students will also understand the social impact of the proposed solutions.
  • Scientific concepts are often taught without addressing the history behind them. History provides a meaningful perspective about scientific concepts, processes and context (Wang & Marsh, 2002). Hence, one may also include a historical perspective in teaching scientific concepts. An example of such integrated learning can be found in a learning unit developed under the Vigyan Pratibha project at Homi Bhabha Centre for Science Education, titled ‘How 'X' got its chemical identity’. The unit discusses how the molecular formula of a commonly used chemical was discovered through observations and experiments of different people, each coming from a different background and place, thus integrating aspects of history and geography to teach a scientific concept, facilitating integrated learning. The unit focuses on inculcating basic skills such as questioning, interpretation, and communication, fostering a scientific temperament in students (Vigyan Pratibha, 2024).
  • Choose a topic and view it from the perspective of multiple subjects. For example, a lesson on the wheel can be explained to students by teaching them where and when the wheel was invented, how different types of wheels are used and made, the significance of mathematical concepts such as radius and circumference with respect to the utility of the wheel as a technological tool, how wheels evolved and how they grew in utility (wheels used for transportation versus potter’s wheel) etc., thus giving them a broader understanding of the topic.

Limitations of integrated learning

While integrated learning offers several advantages, it also has certain limitations. For example, integrated learning while promoting a broader understanding, may hinder the development of specialized skills required for professional courses in the future. Another concern is whether we have enough teachers who can teach in this manner and what kind of training is required to make them effective facilitators in the classroom. Further, teachers may also face challenges in implementing such sessions due to constraints like limited time, lack of expertise in certain subjects, and lack of resources. However, despite these limitations, integrated learning might be an approach worth pursuing in the interest of developing more well-rounded individuals for the future.

Acknowledgement

Deepest gratitude to Prof. Ankush Gupta for providing critical and insightful comments and to Adithi Muralidhar for her meticulous and thoughtful feedback. Their contributions have been invaluable to this work.

References

  1. Nollmeyer, G. E., Kelting-Gibson, L., & Graves, C. J. (2016). Mapping the domain of subject area integration: Elementary educators' descriptions and practices. International Journal of Learning, Teaching and Educational Research, 15(9), 1-27
  2. Niemelä, M. (2022). Subject matter specific curriculum integration: A quantitative study of finnish student teachers’ integrative content knowledge. Journal of Education for Teaching, 48(2), 228-240.
  3. Andrew Fultz, Combining Social Studies and STEM in a Project-Based Learning Unit (2021) https://www.edutopia.org/article/combining-social-studies-and-stem-project-based-learning-unit/
  4. Chung, Y., Yoo, J., Kim, S. W., Lee, H., & Zeidler, D. L. (2016). Enhancing Students’ Communication Skills in the Science Classroom Through SSI. International Journal of Science and Mathematics Education, 14, 1-27. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10763-014-9557-6
  5. Vigyan Pratibha (2024). How “X” got its chemical identity. Retrieved February 05, 2025. https://vigyanpratibha.in/index.php/how-x-got-its-chemical-identity/
  6. Wang, H.A., Marsh, D.D. Science Instruction with a Humanistic Twist: Teachers' Perception and Practice in Using the History of Science in Their Classrooms. Science & Education 11, 169-189 (2002). https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1014455918130
  7. Ivanitskaya, L., Clark, D., Montgomery, G. et al. Interdisciplinary Learning: Process and Outcomes. Innovative Higher Education 27, 95-111 (2002). https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1021105309984

Om Kaustubh Bagwe is a Project Scientific Assistant with the Design and Technology Education Group of the Homi Bhabha Centre for Science Education, TIFR, Mumbai. He has done his post-graduation in microbiology; and is passionate about making science accessible and engaging to children. He can be reached at om@hbcse.tifr.res.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Om Kaustubh Bagwe
Why do we teach subjects in silos when life doesn’t work that way? Om K. Bagwe makes the case for integrating science and social science in school curricula.

item.title: Reimagining geography pedagogy
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sheraz Ahmad Lone and Rouf Ahmad Khan

Geography is often perceived as a subject confined to maps, definitions, and theoretical concepts. But, the subject offers immense scope for moving beyond rote memorization and textbook-centric pedagogy. This article explores how geography education can be transformed through fieldwork, technology integration, and community engagement. It provides practical strategies for teachers to make geography more interactive, relevant, and engaging for students in line with NEP 2020 and NCF guidelines, which advocate a shift towards competency based and experiential learning.

Why move beyond textbooks?

Traditional geography teaching often relies on memorization, making the subject dull and disconnected from real life.  National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 encourages a shift towards critical thinking, hands-on learning, and the use of technology to deepen understanding. Geography directly impacts our daily lives and students need more than just textbooks to understand the subject. By incorporating fieldwork, local case studies, and digital tools, learning becomes engaging, relevant, and meaningful, helping students connect classroom knowledge to the world around them.

Strategies for teaching geography beyond textbooks

Fieldwork and local exploration

Real geography isn't just about reading books, it's about exploring the world around us. When students walk through fields, cross streams, or climb hills, they see geography come to life. Books can explain erosion, but watching soil wash away in the rain makes it real. Field trips change geography from boring facts to exciting discoveries. Walking through a forest helps students feel how weather changes under the trees better than any picture. Seeing plastic waste pile up along a stream/river makes pollution more than just numbers. Even digging in the school garden can teach about soil and rocks.

Bringing geography to life with technology

Technology can turn abstract geography lessons into interactive, hands-on learning experiences that students can see and explore.

  • Google Earth and digital maps: Students can zoom into their local area, compare past and present satellite images, and mark important features like rivers, forests, settlements, and mountains peaks. Platforms like NCERT’s BHUVAN allow students to study detailed maps of India, helping them visualize landscapes and human settlements.
  • Weather apps: Simple weather apps can show real-time temperature, rainfall, and wind patterns. Drones (or even smartphone cameras) can capture aerial views of fields, water bodies, or urban areas, helping students analyze land use and environmental changes.
  • Virtual Reality (VR): If field trips are difficult, VR can take students on digital journeys to mountains, lakes, or deserts. They can "walk" through different terrains, observe erosion patterns, or explore ecosystems--all from the classroom.

These tools make geography more engaging by letting students explore, measure, and interact with real-world data. Instead of just reading about landforms or climate, they can see them, track changes, and even predict future trends-turning them into young geographers.

Community-based learning

Geography isn't just about land and water; it's about how people live with and change their environment. Students can learn directly from their own communities:

  • Talk to farmers: Visit local fields and ask elders how farming has changed. What crops grew 20 years ago? How have rains and temperatures affected harvests? This shows real climate change impacts.
  • Study water systems: Explore how villages traditionally managed water through wells, ponds, or canals. Compare these with today's pipes and pumps. Which methods work better in droughts?
  • Meet disaster volunteers: Work with local groups that prepare for floods or earthquakes. Learn warning systems, safe building tips, and how communities protect themselves.

By learning from farmers, elders, and helpers in their own area, students connect textbook lessons to real people and places.

Project-based learning (PBL)

Students can dive deep into geography by working on simple projects that solve local problems. They could create detailed maps of their local area, marking important features like water sources, farms, and roads while studying how the landscape is changing. Other engaging projects might include investigating pollution in nearby streams, rivers, or fields, interviewing farmers about crop patterns, or analyzing population changes in their community. For practical life skills, students could develop disaster preparedness plans - identifying safe zones during floods or earthquakes and creating emergency kits for their homes or schools. These long-term projects make geography come alive by connecting classroom lessons to students' daily lives, while developing research, teamwork, and problem-solving skills that go beyond textbooks.

Hands-on learning: using clay models to teach geography

Clay models provide a creative and interactive way to learn key geography concepts. Students can shape a spherical clay globe to mark latitudes (like the equator and tropics) and longitudes (such as the prime meridian), helping them visualize Earth’s grid system for locating places. By moulding raised areas for mountains and carving river channels, they can observe how rivers originate in highlands, flow downstream due to gravity, and form drainage patterns. This tactile method enhances spatial understanding, making abstract ideas like coordinates and river systems, the structure of Earth more concrete and engaging for learners.

Challenges and solutions

While these activity-based teaching methods significantly enhance learning, schools often encounter practical hurdles in their implementation. Field trips require careful planning and resources, while access to digital tools like computers and mapping software may be limited. However, schools can overcome these constraints by collaborating with nearby educational institutions, cluster head schools, and local organizations that may share resources and expertise.

The examination-oriented education system sometimes makes it challenging to incorporate hands-on learning. Yet, innovative teachers can seamlessly integrate practical projects with prescribed syllabus topics. Many educators also need support in adopting new technological teaching aids. Fortunately, systematic teacher training programs conducted by SCERTs and DIETs are equipping teachers with necessary digital skills through regular capacity building initiatives.

With these support systems and creative approaches, schools can successfully transition to more engaging, experiential geography education that benefits both students and teachers alike. The key lies in leveraging available resources while adapting methods to local contexts and needs.

Conclusion

Geography education should move beyond textbook learning to include hands-on exploration, digital tools, and real-world connections. By following the NEP 2020 and NCF guidelines, teachers can create engaging lessons where students actively investigate their surroundings. Field trips, local projects, and technology, help students see geography in action. When learners study their own communities/surroundings, they develop practical knowledge and problem-solving skills. This experiential approach not only makes geography more interesting but also prepares students to understand environmental challenges and think of sustainable solutions. By connecting classroom lessons to the real world, we help young geographers become informed citizens who can positively impact their local environments.

Recommendations for teachers

  1. Start small--incorporate one fieldwork/project activity as assessment.
  2. Use free digital tools like Google Earth, NCERT’s BHUVAN.
  3. Collaborate with local experts (farmers, environmentalists).
  4. Document and publish student projects to inspire others.

By reimagining geography education, teachers can nurture a generation of environmentally conscious and analytically skilled citizens.

References:

NEP,2020, https://www.education.gov.in/sites/upload_files/mhrd/files/NEP_Final_English_0.pdf

NCF FS https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/NCF_for_Foundational_Stage_20_October_2022.pdf

Rouf Ahmad Khan is a teacher in the Department of School of Education, JK.

Sheraz Ahmad Lone is is a teacher educator at District Institute of Education and Traings, Anantnag, JK and is also pursuing a doctorate in geography from the Department of Geography, University of Kashmir. The author is also a certified expert in the development of social science textbooks and other curricular materials from NCERT, New Delhi. He holds NET-JRF and PGDDM. He has published about 20 research papers in national and international journals [ springer, Elsevier] and also presented more than 20 papers at a number of national and international conferences. He can be reached at sherazdietang@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sheraz Ahmad Lone and Rouf Ahmad Khan
What does teaching geography mean to you? Map-reading, administering textbook lessons, and providing a fact file? If we want our students to engage with geography, we have to become more creative with our pedagogy. Try introducing field trips, project-based learning, clay modelling, and technology to bring geography alive in your classrooms.

item.title: AI: the new teacher on the block?
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Smitha Prasad

Will AI replace teachers? That’s a question I often hear these days.

Well, by the end of this article, you will find me saying no, it won’t. But AI will transform the role of educators in profound ways.

First, let us answer some questions.

Would you rather talk to a customer service executive or a chatbot to solve your problem?

Would you prefer having a cute little puppy or an AI-enabled robot that behaves like one?

Of course, I would want to speak to a customer service executive and play with a real pup even if it soils my carpets and chews on my shoes!

But why do we still choose the real ones even though the virtual ones work efficiently and give accurate information? The key is emotion. That’s what we look for.

Technology can detect emotions, it can sense moods through sound, facial expressions, choice of words, etc. It might say it understands our feelings, but it’s only programmed to do so…it does not actually feel. It might not be able to differentiate between a sarcastic “wow!” and a “wow” used in appreciation. But as humans, we are smart and sensitive enough to note the difference.

AI can definitely help educators. It can provide extensive information and has the potential to teach and solve problems efficiently. There are numerous AI tools that can help teachers plan lessons, design presentations, analyze student performance and data, and even create reports — while you and I can relax! (Most of these tools come with a fee though!)

Students, too, can use AI tools as their assistants to write creative content, essays, and even complete their homework. As teachers, we often can’t tell whether a piece of work was done by a student or by AI. That leads to an important discussion - is AI really helping students learn or just helping them finish tasks?

While AI can help us in many ways, things get complicated when there is decision-making involved. Technology has no idea what emotions feel like. The behaviour of AI-enabled devices is based on algorithms, past data, and experiences of human beings.

Let’s take a classic example often used to explore decision-making – the train track dilemma!

There are two train tracks, one currently in use and the other unused. A few children are playing on the unused track, but many are playing on the used track as well, even as a train is approaching. What should the driver do? Continue on the used track, risking the lives of many children, or switch to the unused one, where only a few are playing? This is a heart wrenching situation, isn’t it?

If the train were AI-driven, what would it decide? While a human driver might hesitate, torn by emotion, AI would act instantly based on its programming. Its decision would depend on what the programmers thought, what data was fed into it, and countless other factors. What seems emotionally impossible for a human might be a simple calculation for AI.

Now imagine a teacher facing decisions like these!

A teacher’s job is not just to give information to the students, it’s a very complex social-emotional-intellectual relationship, which is a balanced equation comprising teaching, mentoring, monitoring, parenting, disciplining, and empathizing with students. And the equation is different with every child in the class. 

A teacher's day is full of simple and complex situations, which need a lot of analyzing, problem-solving, and decision-making. Sometimes it’s as small as just listening to a child, smiling at them, or noticing their new haircut.

There are times when similar answers are written by two students, I write the remarks as “good job!” for one student and “can do better!” for the other - not because I am biased! But because I know each student’s journey. “Good job!” is for the one who’s improved, and “Can do better!” is for the one I know can push further. When we try to programme a teacher or turn one into a robot with artificial intelligence, there are countless factors to consider — and natural intelligence truly matters!

I keep saying this – yes, definitely AI is the new protagonist, but we are the directors! Our roles as educators may change, and the way we operate may evolve — but machines can never replace humans!

Smitha Prasad is an electronics and communications engineer, working for the Brigade Foundation, Bengaluru. She is a curriculum advisor for the Department of Computer Science. She can be reached at smithagprasad@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Smitha Prasad
The core of a teacher-student relationship is emotions. Yes, exchange of knowledge, sharing of information, and guiding students is a teacher’s primary responsibility. But these are things that a well-developed AI tool can do as well. In this day and age what separates a human teacher from AI is her ability to connect with her students emotionally.

item.title: How the school system is struggling to survive in Banswara
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Shishu Ranjan

Banswara is often called the “Cherapunji of Rajasthan” because it receives the highest rainfall in the state. As a result, it is also greener than most places in Rajasthan. It is believed that Banswara gets its name from the abundance of bamboo (bans in Hindi) forests in the area.

Banswara is home to many tribal communities, such as the Bhils, Bhil Meenas, Damor, Charpotas, and Ninamas. The tribes practice different traditions following different gurus. While Hinduisim and Christianity are trying to make inroads, so far the tribes have managed to preserve their practices and cultures.

The tribal people in Banswara celebrate two unique festivals. One is called Dewra. It is celebrated by theBhils and Meenas. During this festival, people make idols of family members who have passed away and perform rituals in their honour. The other is called Notra.This is like a fund raising event, where lunch is organized by a family that needsmoney or resources and members from the community come partake of the lunch and offer their support in whatever way they can.

Agriculture is the main occupation and the language spoken is Wagri, which is considered to be a mixture of Gujrati and Mewari. Most of the population from remote Banswara is also dependent on seasonal migration. Parents migrate to Malwa leaving younger kids at home with older siblings or grandparents. Some people also migrate to states as far as Kerala to work as construction labourers.

Health is a concern in this district. Infant mortality is high. 82% of the children under the age of 5 are anemic and so are 43% of the women (15-49). Girls are married under the age of 18 and give birth to at least one child by the age of 18.

Apart from tribal people, other castes that reside in this region are Patels/Patidars, Yadavs, Rajputs, Brahmans, Mahajans, and Muslims. Caste-based discrimination is pervasive in the region. Not just among the adults, but even at school, the Gurjar and Sevak students consider themselves superior and bully the tribal students both inside and outside classrooms.

Teaching and learning

As part of my internship with Azim Premji Foundation, I visited a few schools in the district. While there are also schools in very remote areas, since there is no proper road to reach them, my fellow interns and I only visited schools that were close to the district headquarters in Banswara. Below, I present my assessment of the educational environment in this region.

Infrastructure

We visited eight schools, in all of which water leakage/seepage issues were prominent. The upkeep of the school buildings was dismal with ceilings looking like they might collapse any moment. Yet, children were sitting in these classrooms, as they had no choice. In one school, we found that out of six classrooms, only four were usable.

There was not a single school where we found usable toilets for girls. There was one school where there was a functional toilet, but the taps were broken, and there was no proper water connection. The UDISE portal, however, proudly mentions that every school in the district has functional toilets for both boys and girls.

Of the eight schools, only four had boundary walls.

Teacher’s role

PEEOs (Panchayat Elementary Education Officer responsible for Panchayat level Education) are overburdened with teaching and non-academic tasks assigned by the government. They have to undertake surveys for the government, immunization campaigns, animal surveys, etc. Apart from all this, they also have to participate in academic training provided by the DIETs and other organizations. They become the foot soldiers of every scheme the government rolls out at the Panchayat level.

Teachers in the district face challenges in teaching because of the following reasons:

  • Language: Some of the teachers who are from other districts do not know Wagri, and therefore find it difficult to communicate with and teach their students.
  • Seasonal migration: Because of this, teachers face challenges in bringing students back to school. Adolescents migrate with their parents, creating a learning gap.
  • Lack of human resources: Due to this, teachers have to manage everything themselves, which causes a big gap in effective teaching.
  • Lack of community engagement in schools: In remote areas, we found that communities don’t take education seriously due to a lack of awareness and constant migration for work.
  • Aggressive behaviour of teachers: Teachers from dominant castes often discriminate against tribal students, which disturbs harmony in the classroom.
  • Child labour: Child labour is a common phenomenon among tribes; adolescents migrate with their parents and help them in their work, leaving their education behind. There are instances of child trafficking as well. We came to know from a government report that the district is prone to child trafficking.

While this picture appears bleak and dismal, there are good stories too, albeit few.

In one of the upper primary schools that we visited, we found a proactive teacher, who took it upon himself to repair the school building. With the help of some CSR funds, NGO support, and Notra, he restored the building. This school also had functional toilets. The classrooms were appropriately furnished and decorated. They were also well-stocked with books for children.

This teacher also organized a “Research Mela” for his students, where they researched about the history of their village. The teacher documented and recorded the efforts of his students. Azim Premji Foundation played a crucial role helping him in this process.

Problematizing the context

Banswara is one the most backward regions in Rajasthan. Teachers avoid being posted here. If unavoidable, teachers prefer schools closer to the district headquarters. With very few people from the local community coming forward to teach, teachers from other districts have to be recruited. The district has a few teacher-education institutions, but they are in degraded conditions. The District Institute of Education and Training is running under severe shortage of lecturers and resources. One government official even said that “DIET is dead”. This poses a big question to the education system of a tribal district, where teachers need to be trained well so that they can be sensitive towards children who are under the constant threat of migration, child labour, malnutrition, child trafficking, caste-based discrimination, etc.

It is a well-known fact that teacher education is in a precarious condition across the country. Now, the question that arises is whether our teachers are prepared to teach such children and what kind of efforts are being made by the education department beyond the conventional in-service training to prepare teachers for social justice. What efforts are being made to reduce caste-based discrimination in schools? Has social justice in teacher education been made part of research in the Indian context, where the complexity of inequality exists in multiple layers?

(This article is a summary and reflection of the internship report submitted as part of an assignment.)

Shishu Ranjan is a Chevening Scholar (2024-25). He studied MSc Education and Social Justice at Birkbeck, University of London. He has completed his MA in Education from Azim Premji University and BEd from Jamia Millia Islamia. He is interested in teacher education and social justice. He can be reached at shishu.ranjan22_mae@apu.edu.in / s.ranjan.bbk@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shishu Ranjan
Witnessing life and schooling in Banswara, one of Rajasthan’s most backward districts, Shishu Ranjan captures the everyday realities of teaching and learning.
